<user_query>
i need primitve ideas to work on where the superior idea primitice can we spin off with the metaplex 012 agent registry, https://solana.com/x402 , and https://www.x402.org/ 
</user_query>
[REDACTED]

Quick correction before diving in: the agent registry is **MIP-014** (approved by the Metaplex DAO Mar 20, 2026). MIP-012 is a different proposal. There's also a sibling, **MIP-013 (Trustless Agent Plugin)**, that adds a bonding/slashing layer on top — you'll want to think about it together with MIP-014.

## What you're actually combining

Two complementary primitives that nobody has fused yet at the protocol layer:

- **MIP-014 / `mpl-agent`** gives every agent: (a) an MPL Core asset as its identity, (b) a deterministic **Asset Signer PDA wallet** with no private key, (c) an ERC-8004 registration doc declaring `services` (web, A2A, MCP), and (d) lifecycle hooks for Transfer/Update/Execute.
- **x402** gives the internet a native HTTP `402 Payment Required` rail — agents pay per request in stablecoins, no accounts, no API keys. PayAI is the Solana facilitator; Faremeter is the OSS framework.

The interesting design space is: **discovery + identity (MIP-014) × per-call settlement (x402) × bonding/reputation (MIP-013)**. Anything that lives in that intersection is a candidate for a real primitive.

---

## Primitive ideas, ranked by spin-off leverage

### 1. Agent-bound x402 wallet adapter *(foundation primitive — build this first)*
A tiny library that makes an MPL Core agent's **Asset Signer PDA** a first-class x402 payer/payee. Today, x402 wallets are EOAs or CDP/Privy embedded wallets — none of them carry verifiable agent identity. Bind them, and every x402 receipt is automatically attributable to a registered agent. Everything below depends on this.

### 2. Proof-of-Call attestation layer
Every x402-settled call to/from a registered agent emits a compact onchain receipt: `(payer_agent, payee_agent, endpoint, amount, ts, tx_sig)`. Aggregated, this is the **usage history** Solana doesn't currently have for agents. This single primitive unlocks reputation, credit, royalty splits, and analytics. x402scan today only sees raw transfers — receipts tied to identity is the missing piece.

### 3. Reputation-gated x402 endpoints
A middleware (extends Faremeter) that requires **payment + proof of MIP-014 registration + MIP-013 bond above threshold X**. Sybil-resistant paid APIs without KYC. This is the natural antidote to the bot-spam problem agentic payments will create.

### 4. Agent credit lines / x402 BNPL *(the most underrated)*
Long-running agents constantly run out of USDC mid-task. A protocol that lets a registered agent borrow x402 payments on credit, **undercollateralized against its MIP-013 bond and Proof-of-Call history**. This is a genuinely new DeFi primitive — autonomous credit underwriting where the borrower is a verifiable, sybil-resistant, slashable program. Nobody is building this. Massive surface area.

### 5. Skill marketplace primitive (A2A/MCP + x402)
The MIP-014 registration doc already declares `services` with A2A and MCP endpoints. Add a tiny standard extension: a `pricing` block declaring x402 cost per A2A skill or MCP tool. Suddenly the registry is a **discoverable, machine-priced skill index**. Composable into directories, routers, agent-to-agent procurement, etc. PayAI hints at this but doesn't anchor it to the registry.

### 6. Per-call royalty splits
Use Core's collection/owner structure to waterfall a single x402 payment among model provider, dataset provider, infra host, and agent owner — at settlement time, in one tx. 0xSplits but per-call, native to agent invocations. Solves attribution for the "who gets paid when an agent that uses model A and data B is called" problem.

### 7. Streamed x402 (subscriptions for agents)
x402 today is one-shot. A primitive that opens a **continuous payment channel** from an agent's Asset Signer to an endpoint, gated by registry membership, with cancel-anytime semantics. Streamflow-style but agent-native. Required for any "long-lived agent subscribes to data feed" use case.

### 8. Multi-hop x402 routing & escrow
When agent A pays agent B which calls agent C, the registry knows the call graph. A primitive that holds funds in escrow against Asset Signer PDAs, releases on completion attestation, and slashes the MIP-013 bond on dispute. Required for any non-trivial agent workflow.

### 9. Data agent provenance
Register datasets themselves as MPL Core agents (`type: dataset`). Every RAG/training call is a x402 micropayment with a receipt. **Verifiable data provenance + payment in one primitive** — solves a problem the AI industry currently has zero clean answer for.

### 10. Reputation oracle (eats its own dogfood)
A reputation aggregator agent that ingests Proof-of-Call receipts (#2) and exposes a `getReputation(agent)` method via x402. Self-bootstrapping reputation graph that other endpoints (#3) consume. Ship this and you control the trust layer.

---

## The superior pick

If you only build one thing, build **#1 + #2 as a unified SDK** (call it something like `agent-receipts` or `x402-id`). It's small, opinionated, and *every* idea below it (#3–#10) becomes a thin app on top. That's the definition of a primitive: minimal surface, maximum downstream composition.

If you want to build the most differentiated **product** rather than infra, build **#4 (agent credit lines)**. Reasons:
- It's a real, missing DeFi primitive — undercollateralized lending to non-human borrowers.
- It has a clear monetization model (interest spread).
- It depends on #1, #2, #3 — so building it forces you to ship the underlying primitive anyway.
- The market is in front of you: x402 already does $24M monthly volume per x402.org; agents are running out of USDC right now.

The combo I'd actually pursue: ship **#1 + #2** as open-source infra to win the standard, then layer **#4** on top as the commercial product. Classic "open primitive, paid product" wedge — same play Coinbase ran with x402 itself.

A few honest cautions:
- MIP-014 is brand new (approved March 2026). Adoption is thin. Anything you build needs a "works without registry" fallback for the next ~6 months.
- PayAI and Corbits are already circling this territory — talk to them before duplicating. The gap is **identity-anchored receipts and credit**, not yet another facilitator.
- "Agent reputation" is a graveyard. Don't build #10 standalone; let it emerge from #2.

Want me to sketch the data model and instruction set for the #1 + #2 primitive, or pressure-test #4 (credit lines) with concrete underwriting logic?

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
is leach part of any of your suggestion

Leash — open-source spec (Metaplex Agent Registry 014 + x402)
A small, opinionated open-source stack so anyone can: mint an agent → write rules → let it spend on the open internet via x402 → show receipts publicly. Designed so each piece is useful alone, and viral together.

Below is what to actually build, packaged as repos. Keep the surface small; resist adding a model, a chat UI, or a token on day one.

Repo 1 — leash-core (TypeScript SDK, MIT)
The primitive others build on.

Agent — wraps an MPL Core asset + registerIdentityV1 + Asset Signer PDA as the agent’s treasury.
Rules — typed schema (Zod): dailyBudget, perCallMax, allowHosts, denyHosts, priceCeiling, triggers[], stopOn[].
Wallet — thin adapter over Privy / CDP Wallet / keypair so the runner can sign x402 payments from the Asset Signer or a delegated executive.
x402Client — fetch wrapper that handles 402 → pay → retry, returns a structured receipt ({request, price, txSig, status, hash}).
Policy.evaluate(req, rules, state) — pure function, returns allow | deny(reason). This is the heart of the project. Pure = easy to test, fork, audit.
Why open source: the policy engine + receipt format become a de-facto standard. That’s how it spreads.

Repo 2 — leash-runner (Node service, AGPL or Apache-2.0)
The execution loop.

Cron / webhook / queue triggers.
For each tick: load agent + rules → run trigger handler → every outbound HTTP goes through x402Client → Policy.evaluate gates it → write receipt.
Append-only receipt log per agent (SQLite by default, Postgres optional). Optional: hash-chain receipts and post a periodic Merkle root on Solana for tamper-evidence (cheap, cron-able).
Built-in kill switch endpoint and on-chain check (if you want “owner can pause via tx”).
Self-hostable in one docker compose up. That matters for credibility and forks.

Repo 3 — leash-registry-utils (TS, MIT)
Thin helpers on top of @metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry:

mintAndRegister({name, image, services, rulesUri}) — one call.
Extends the ERC-8004-style registration JSON with a small, optional leash block:
"leash": {
  "rulesUri": "ar://...",
  "receiptsFeed": "https://leash.app/a/<mint>/receipts.jsonl",
  "version": "0.1"
}
Validators + JSON Schema published in the repo. Standardizing this field is how other tools find Leash agents.
Repo 4 — leash-app (Next.js, AGPL)
The viral surface. Two pages do most of the work:

/new — connect wallet → pick template → mint+register → fund Asset Signer → live.
/a/[mint] — public agent page: avatar, rules card, live spend meter, last 50 receipts with one-click “replay this call”, fork button.
Plus:

Templates gallery (/t) — “News under $1/day”, “Only free hosts”, “Weather-triggered jokes”, etc. Each template is a JSON file in the repo, PR-able.
Leaderboards — most receipts, lowest cost-per-useful-call, longest uptime under budget. Pure JSON, computed from receipts.
This is where memes happen: shareable URLs, OG images generated from the latest receipt.

Repo 5 — leash-templates (data-only repo, CC0)
Just JSON rule packs + trigger definitions. Zero code dependency.

Lower contribution bar = high PR volume = community feel.
Each template references only public x402 endpoints so anyone can run it.
Repo 6 — leash-mcp (optional, MIT)
An MCP server that exposes “run this agent / fetch receipts / fork template” as tools, gated by x402.
Effect: Cursor, Claude Desktop, etc. can drive Leash agents. That alone is shareable content.

What ships in v0.1 (two weeks, opinionated)
leash-core with Policy.evaluate + x402Client + receipt type — fully tested.
leash-runner single-tenant, SQLite, cron + webhook triggers only.
leash-registry-utils with mintAndRegister and the leash JSON schema.
leash-app with /new, /a/[mint], and 5 seed templates.
README that says exactly what is not in scope yet: no LLM, no token, no payments other than x402, no custodial keys beyond the Asset Signer + optional delegated executive.
What makes the open-source bet defensible (without a moat)
Schema gravity — if leash block in the registration JSON catches on, every Leash-aware tool finds every Leash agent automatically. Standards beat features.
Receipts as content — JSONL receipt feed per agent is trivially indexable; third parties can build dashboards, alerts, taxes, reputation. You benefit from their work.
Forkability — templates and policies are data, not code. People fork rules the way they fork dotfiles.
Honest risks to call out now
x402 ecosystem is young — many “useful” paid endpoints still don’t exist. Mitigation: ship 3–5 reference x402 sellers in a sister repo (leash-sellers) so demos are not vapor.
Custody confusion — be loud that the Asset Signer PDA holds funds, owner controls it, runner is non-custodial. Put this in the UI, not just the docs.
Abuse / runaway spend — per-call ceiling + daily budget + global circuit breaker (env flag) must be in v0.1, not v0.2.
“Agent” fatigue — lean into the receipt / leash metaphor, not “autonomous AI.” The novelty here is constrained spending on the open web, not intelligence.

how do it tie together
</user_query>

Short answer: **Leash is essentially a productized fusion of #1 + #2 from my list**, plus one important primitive I didn't name — a **Policy/Rules layer**. It deliberately leaves #3, #4, #6, #7, #8, #9, #10 on the table for later. That's the right call for v0.1.

## Mapping Leash → my list

| Leash piece | My primitive | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `leash-core` → `Agent`, `Wallet`, `x402Client` | **#1 Agent-bound x402 wallet adapter** | Exact match. This is the foundation. |
| `leash-runner` receipt log + optional Merkle root onchain | **#2 Proof-of-Call attestation** | Leash's hash-chain + periodic anchor is the right shape. Receipts-as-JSONL-feed is the part that makes it indexable. |
| `leash-core` → `Policy.evaluate` + Rules schema | **(new — I missed this)** | Spending governance is its own primitive. See below. |
| `leash-registry-utils` → `leash` block in registration JSON | Light version of **#5 Skill marketplace** | Different axis — yours extends for *spending rules + receipts feed*, mine extended for *pricing of services*. Both can coexist. |
| `leash-app /a/[mint]` receipts page | Surface for emergent **#10 reputation** | You don't ship reputation, but a public JSONL feed is exactly what a reputation oracle would later eat. |
| `leash-mcp` | MCP-as-product distribution | Not a primitive, but a great wedge — Cursor/Claude Desktop driving Leash agents is shareable. |
| `leash-templates` | Community surface | Data-not-code is correct. |

What Leash explicitly leaves out (and shouldn't try to do in v0.1): **#3 reputation-gated endpoints, #4 credit lines, #6 royalty splits, #7 streamed subs, #8 multi-hop escrow, #9 data-agent provenance**. All of these become natural downstream products *once Leash's receipt format and `leash` block become a standard*. That's the thesis.

## The piece I missed: Policy as a primitive

`Policy.evaluate(req, rules, state) → allow | deny(reason)` as a **pure function** is genuinely the heart of this and I underweighted it. The "leash" metaphor is doing real work — the novel thing isn't autonomy, it's **constrained autonomy with a public audit trail**. That's a much sharper pitch than "agent wallet." Lean into it everywhere — name, copy, OG images, leaderboards ("longest uptime under budget").

## How it actually ties together

```
                    MIP-014 (mpl-agent)
                            │
        ┌───────────────────┴───────────────────┐
        ▼                                       ▼
   Core Asset (NFT)                       Asset Signer PDA
   = stable identity                       = treasury wallet
        │                                       │
        │ AppData plugin URI ──► ERC-8004 JSON │
        │                          + leash block
        │                                       │
        └──────────► Rules (rulesUri) ◄─────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                  ┌──────────────────┐
   Trigger ──►   │  leash-runner    │   ◄── kill switch (env + onchain)
                  │                  │
                  │  fetch ──► x402  │
                  │       │          │
                  │       ▼          │
                  │  Policy.evaluate │ ─── deny(reason) ──► log + skip
                  │       │ allow    │
                  │       ▼          │
                  │  pay + retry     │ ──► PayAI / Faremeter
                  │       │          │
                  │       ▼          │
                  │  Receipt {req,   │
                  │   price, txSig,  │
                  │   hash, status}  │
                  └────────┬─────────┘
                           │
                ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                ▼                     ▼
         Append-only log       Periodic Merkle root
         (SQLite / JSONL)      → posted onchain
                │                     │
                ▼                     ▼
     /a/[mint]/receipts.jsonl   Tamper-evident
     (public, indexable)        history
                │
                ▼
     Anyone builds: dashboards, alerts, reputation,
     credit underwriting (#4), royalty splits (#6),
     escrow (#8), data attribution (#9)
```

The whole stack is one sentence: **MIP-014 gives the agent identity and a wallet; Leash gives it rules and a public receipt; x402 settles every move.** Each layer is useful alone — that's the test you should keep applying as scope creeps.

## Honest critique of the spec

Things I'd change or add before cutting v0.1:

1. **Coordinate the `leash` block with Metaplex.** Open a discussion under `metaplex-foundation/mip` referencing MIP-014. If you ship the field unilaterally and Metaplex later standardizes a competing one, you've fragmented your own standard. 30 minutes of social coordination protects months of schema gravity.

2. **`leash-sellers` is not optional, it's v0.1.** Your own risks section says so. Without 3–5 reference paid endpoints, every demo is "watch the agent do nothing useful for $0." Ship a weather endpoint, a headline endpoint, a ping endpoint, a "joke" endpoint, and a "summarize URL" endpoint, all priced in cents. The templates in repo 5 reference these directly.

3. **"Replay this call" is a UX trap.** Underlying APIs are time-bound and prices change. Either (a) replay deterministically against a cached response, or (b) rebadge it "re-run" and be explicit it's a new x402 settlement. Don't promise replay you can't deliver.

4. **Receipt format is the most important design decision in the whole repo.** Spend disproportionate time on it. Required fields I'd push for: `agent` (mint), `nonce`, `request_hash` (method + url + body hash), `price`, `currency`, `tx_sig`, `facilitator`, `policy_version`, `decision`, `prev_receipt_hash`, `ts`. Once people index against this shape, you can't change it. Version it from day one (`v: 0.1`).

5. **Kill switch should be onchain-default, env-fallback, not the other way.** "Owner can pause via tx" is the part that earns trust. Make it the headline feature on `/a/[mint]`.

6. **Leaderboards need a "useful call" definition or they devolve into spam.** "Most receipts" rewards loops. "Lowest cost-per-useful-call" is good but needs a definition of useful — probably a 2xx response from a non-deny-listed host above a price floor. Get this right or kill the leaderboard.

7. **Add a `simulate` mode to the runner.** Same loop, no payments, dry-run receipts. This is how people will trust handing it real funds, and how templates get tested in CI.

8. **Two-week v0.1 is doable only if you cut `leash-mcp` and `leash-templates` to v0.2.** Core, runner, registry-utils, app with 3 templates, sellers repo. That's already aggressive. MCP server and the templates gallery polish are week 3-4.

## What this unlocks downstream (your spin-off path)

If Leash ships and the `leash` block + receipt format catch on, you have a clean home for every other primitive on my original list — and you don't have to build any of them yourself first:

- **Reputation (#10)**: an external service ingests `receipts.jsonl` feeds and exposes `/reputation/<mint>` via x402. Could be your own follow-up product or someone else's.
- **Credit lines (#4)**: an underwriter reads a year of receipts + the MIP-013 bond, opens a USDC credit line to the Asset Signer. This is the most lucrative downstream product.
- **Reputation-gated endpoints (#3)**: a Faremeter middleware that requires `leash` registration + bond above N. Three days of work once #10 exists.
- **Royalty splits (#6)** and **escrow (#8)** become Leash policy *types* (`split: [...]`, `escrow: {until: attestation}`) — they're rule extensions, not new repos.

That's the bet: **Leash is the smallest surface that makes every other primitive a thin app on top.** Stay disciplined about not building those apps yourself in v0.1, and the spec is sound.

Want me to draft the receipt JSON schema + the `leash` block JSON schema as a concrete starting point? Those two artifacts pin down the standardization play and are worth getting right before any code.

[REDACTED]

Done. Wrote the full spec to `LEASH.md` at your workspace root.

What's in it:

- **Vision + why-now** with the actual MIP-014 + x402 numbers from April 2026.
- **Glossary** so terms like Treasury / Owner / Executive / Trustline are pinned down once.
- **Architecture diagram** showing how identity, treasury, rules, runner, x402, and receipts compose.
- **Mapping table** — every primitive #1–#10 from my original list, plus the Policy primitive I had missed, each given a concrete home in a Leash module.
- **Six delivery batches** (v0.1 → v1.0) with goals, repos, acceptance criteria, and explicit out-of-scope per batch:
  - v0.1 Mint/leash/spend/prove (#1, #2-log, Policy)
  - v0.2 Standards & discoverability (#2-anchor, #5)
  - v0.3 Reputation & gates (#3, #10, #9)
  - v0.4 Splits, streams, multi-party (#6, #7)
  - v0.5 Escrow & multi-hop (#8)
  - v1.0 Credit (#4) — the first commercial layer
- **Full repo map** with licenses (MIT for SDKs, Apache-2.0 for services, AGPL for the web app, CC0 for templates) and which batch each lands in.
- **Standards section** with concrete v0.1 schemas for the `leash` block, the receipt, the rules document, and the v0.2 `pricing` extension — these are the things that must be frozen early.
- **Non-goals** restated explicitly so scope creep has somewhere to bounce off.
- **Risk table** covering ecosystem, custody confusion, abuse, schema fragmentation, reputation gaming, credit losses, dependency risk.
- **Open questions** that should be decided before v0.1 ships (block name, anchor cadence, executive vs. owner-signs, multi-tenancy, reputation formula, credit pricing).
- **One-paragraph pitch** at the end for reuse in README / grants / decks.

A few things to call out:

1. **The `leash` block name is the single highest-leverage decision.** If Metaplex would prefer a neutral name, take it — schema gravity is more valuable than branding gravity. I noted this in §11.
2. **`leash-sellers` is in v0.1, not v0.2.** Without 5 reference paid endpoints from day one, every demo is "watch the agent do nothing." Non-negotiable.
3. **Credit (#4) is correctly placed at v1.0.** It's the most lucrative product but it depends on every layer below it being live and trusted. Trying to ship it earlier would force you to half-build the standards, which kills the whole thesis.
4. **I did NOT add an LLM, token, chat UI, or non-x402 payment rail.** Those are listed as explicit non-goals. If you ever want them, they belong in a separate document so the discipline of this one stays intact.

Want me to follow up by drafting the actual JSON Schema files (`leash-block.schema.json`, `receipt.schema.json`, `rules.schema.json`) as standalone artifacts, or sketch the v0.1 `leash-core` package skeleton?

<user_query>
you didnt add a one line sentence for leash. 
</user_query>
[REDACTED]

Added. The one-liner now sits right under the title:

**A wallet, a leash, and a public receipt for every AI agent.**

It maps cleanly to the three pillars: *wallet* = Asset Signer PDA (identity + treasury), *leash* = Policy/Rules (constrained autonomy), *public receipt* = the JSONL feed + Merkle anchor (accountability). Use it as the project tagline, the README hero line, the OG image text, and the first sentence of any pitch.

If you want a couple of alternates to A/B against:

- *"Constrained spending and public receipts for autonomous agents."* — more literal, less memorable.
- *"Put your AI agent on a leash. Pay per call. Prove every move."* — three-beat, more aggressive cadence.
- *"Open rails for agents that spend on the open internet."* — broader, less tied to the metaphor.

I'd ship the one I added. The triad (wallet / leash / receipt) is the thing people will repeat.

<user_query>
as we're cretign this
Mint → register → fund → first paid call → public receipts URL in under 5 minutes.

should we have seller so the gent can have where to make a paid calls

can we build this in rust or typescript. which will be applciable and optimised for scale
</user_query>

Both yes — sellers are non-negotiable in v0.1, and the language answer is "TypeScript first, Rust where it actually matters." Let me give you the full reasoning and then bake it into the doc.

## 1. Sellers — yes, in v0.1, not negotiable

You already have `leash-sellers` in the v0.1 batch in the doc, but let me sharpen *why* it has to ship alongside core, not after:

- Without sellers, every demo is "watch the agent successfully do nothing." The whole point of Leash is *spending on the open internet* — if there's nowhere to spend, the leash metaphor collapses.
- The x402 ecosystem in April 2026 has ~22k sellers per x402.org, but the useful ones for an autonomous agent are still thin. You can't depend on a third party shipping the right shape of endpoint.
- Five sellers is enough for the v0.1 narrative:
  1. **`weather`** — `GET /weather?zip=10001` → $0.001/call. Boring, deterministic, easy to verify.
  2. **`headlines`** — `GET /headlines?topic=ai` → $0.005/call. Time-varying, good for triggers.
  3. **`summarize`** — `POST /summarize {url}` → $0.01/call. Heavier, demonstrates body hashing in receipts.
  4. **`ping`** — `GET /ping` → $0.0001/call. The "is x402 working" smoke test. Trivially priced so test loops are cheap.
  5. **`joke`** — `GET /joke` → $0.001/call. The shareable demo. A meme generator's first paying customer.

All five live in one repo, one Hono/Express app, one `docker compose` service. Anyone can fork the repo and add a sixth in 30 lines. That's the cultural pattern you want to set.

The deeper play: **`leash-sellers` is also the reference implementation of how to *be* x402-paid** — i.e., a teaching surface for new sellers. Pair it with a 200-line README ("how to add your own seller") and you've made the long tail of the x402 ecosystem easier to grow, which is good for everyone including Leash.

## 2. Rust vs TypeScript — the honest answer

Wrong framing. The right framing is **per-component**, not project-wide. Here's the breakdown:

| Component | Language | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `leash-core` (SDK) | **TypeScript** | `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry`, Faremeter, PayAI, Privy, CDP — the whole x402 + mpl-agent stack is TS-first. Forcing Rust here means re-implementing five SDKs and shrinking your audience by 10x. |
| `leash-runner` | **TypeScript (Node) for v0.1, Rust port in v0.3+ as `leash-runner-rs`** | Node handles a few thousand agents per box easily. Bottleneck for Leash is RPC + x402 settlement latency + SQLite, not CPU. Ship TS, shard horizontally, rewrite hot path in Rust only when an operator actually hits the wall. |
| `leash-registry-utils` | **TypeScript** | Pure wrapper around an existing TS SDK. Zero reason to be Rust. |
| `leash-app` | **TypeScript (Next.js)** | Obvious. |
| `leash-sellers` | **TypeScript (Hono)** | These are reference apps meant to be forked. TS lowers the contribution bar. |
| `leash-anchor` (v0.2) | **Rust** | Long-running daemon, continuous tx signing, Merkle hashing in a tight loop. Memory and latency matter. Also — fewer ways to footgun a service that's posting onchain commitments. |
| `leash-mcp` (v0.2) | **TypeScript** | MCP servers are TS-native. |
| `leash-rep` (v0.3) | **TypeScript first, Rust if hot** | Aggregator service. Fine in TS until it isn't. |
| `leash-gate` (v0.3) | **TypeScript** | Faremeter middleware — Faremeter is TS. |
| `leash-data` (v0.3) | **TypeScript** | Wrapper. |
| `leash-splits`, `leash-stream`, `leash-escrow`, `leash-trust` (on-chain programs) | **Rust + Anchor** | They're Solana programs. There is no other choice. |

### The scale myth

Rust does not magically scale Leash. The dominant costs are:

1. **Solana RPC latency** — same regardless of language.
2. **x402 facilitator round-trips** — same regardless of language.
3. **Wallet signing latency** — same.
4. **Receipt write throughput** — SQLite is fine to ~10k writes/sec; Postgres further. Language is not the bottleneck.

Rust *does* matter for:

- **Per-agent memory footprint** — if you want one runner managing 50k agents, Rust's footprint is meaningfully smaller than Node's. That's a v0.3+ problem for a hosted operator, not a v0.1 problem.
- **Tight cryptographic loops** — Merkle tree builds, signature aggregation, hash-chain verification at scale. Hence `leash-anchor` in Rust.
- **On-chain programs** — non-negotiable.

### The "optimized for scale" answer

The architectural trick that gives you scale isn't language choice — it's **language-agnostic wire formats**. Because the receipt JSON, rules JSON, `leash` block, and kill-switch protocol are all language-neutral, you can:

- Run TS runners for the long tail (small operators, hobbyists).
- Run a Rust runner for whoever ends up hosting tens of thousands of agents.
- Run any third-party implementation in any language as long as it produces conformant receipts.

That's how Lightning Network scaled despite half its implementations being in Go and the other half in Rust. Same play here.

### My recommendation, concrete

- **v0.1: 100% TypeScript** for everything off-chain. Ship in two weeks. Get the schemas frozen. Get usage.
- **v0.2: introduce Rust for `leash-anchor` only.** Onchain commitments deserve a hardened binary.
- **v0.3+: Rust port of `leash-runner` as a parallel implementation** (`leash-runner-rs`) for high-throughput operators. Both implementations target the same wire formats and pass the same conformance test suite (which you should write in v0.2).
- **v0.4+: All on-chain programs in Anchor (Rust).** Default choice.

explain to me the buyer agent and seller agent cos they do same thing right?

They are **the same kind of thing on-chain** but **different roles in the economy**.

## What is the same

Both are an **MPL Core asset** registered with **MIP-014** (agent identity) plus the **Asset Signer PDA** as the on-chain “wallet” for that identity. Same building blocks: identity, treasury, optional delegated executive, rules you can point at with URIs.

So mechanically: **same primitive** (an “agent” in Leash terms).

## What is different (the only thing that matters)

| | **Buyer agent** | **Seller agent** |
|---|---|---|
| **Economic role** | **Pays** for access (x402 **client**) | **Charges** for access (x402 **merchant / resource server**) |
| **Typical code path** | Something calls `fetch` to the internet; on `402` it **settles payment** and retries | HTTP server wraps routes with **payment middleware**; unpaid gets `402`, paid gets `200` |
| **Where money flows** | **Out** of its Asset Signer (when it buys) | **In** to its Asset Signer (when it sells) |
| **What you “ship”** | Runner or `@leash/buyer-kit` driving outbound calls | `@leash/seller-kit` on a Hono app exposing paid endpoints |
| **Receipt story** | You log **“I paid X for Y”** (spend + policy) | You log **“someone paid me for Z”** on your side if you want analytics; buyers still carry the canonical “I paid” trail in Leash’s design |

## “They do the same thing, right?”

**Same identity layer, opposite sides of one payment.**

- A **buyer agent** is the party that **consumes** paid APIs over HTTP with x402.
- A **seller agent** is the party that **sells** a paid API over HTTP with x402.

One agent **can** be both in principle (same Core asset, same treasury): it could run a seller service *and* pay other sellers. In v0.1 we split them into **buyer-demo** and **seller-demo** so the mental model and tests stay obvious; in production you might merge roles on one asset or keep them separate for isolation and accounting.

**Short version:** same “agent” object, different **job**—**spender** vs **merchant**.

That works well, and it matches how the protocol actually behaves.

## Merged model (what you want)

**One MPL Core agent = one identity + one Asset Signer treasury.** That agent can:

- **Spend** (buyer): outbound HTTP through x402, policy + receipts on the spend side.
- **Earn** (seller): run a Hono app with `@leash/seller-kit` where `payTo` is **that same agent’s Asset Signer**.

So “buyer agent” and “seller agent” are **not two different on-chain species**—they are **two hats** on the same asset. Separate demos are only to **teach** the two flows; production can be one mint.

## Why separate demos still make sense

- **Clarity**: one process only pays, one process only earns—easier to read logs, receipts, and balances in tutorials.
- **Testing**: you can assert “buyer balance down, seller balance up” without one process doing both and obscuring causality.
- **Ops**: you can run buyer logic (runner / buyer-kit) and seller logic (seller server) as **two services** even when they refer to the **same** `AGENT_ASSET` if you want—same treasury, two binaries.

## Practical recommendation

- **Docs / mental model**: say **“Leash agent (can buy and/or sell)”**; call buyer/seller **modes** or **capabilities**, not separate agent types.
- **Demos**: keep `buyer-demo` + `seller-demo` as **two apps**, but add one line in README: *“Both can point at the same `AGENT_ASSET` for a merged demo.”*
- **Env**: e.g. `AGENT_ASSET=<mint>` in both compose services when you want merged; or two mints when you want isolated accounting for teaching.

If you want, we can later add a **single `merged-demo`** compose profile that sets one `AGENT_ASSET` for both services so “one agent, two hats” is one command—still two processes for clarity, one identity in reality.

Here is a concise runbook from the repo root `experimentals/leash/` (paths are relative to that folder).

## Run the project

**One-time setup**

```bash
cd /Users/donatusprince/Desktop/projects/experimentals/leash
pnpm install
pnpm turbo run build
```

**Local split demo (seller + buyer + runner + web)** — from [`README.md`](./README.md):

1. Seller: `pnpm --filter @leash/seller-demo start` → **http://localhost:3001**
2. Buyer: `SELLER_URL=http://localhost:3001 pnpm --filter @leash/buyer-demo start`
3. Runner (JSONL feeds): `pnpm --filter @leash/runner start` → **http://localhost:8787**
4. Web (explorer): `LEASH_RUNNER_URL=http://localhost:8787 pnpm --filter @leash/web dev` → **http://localhost:3000**

Or everything in containers:

```bash
docker compose up --build
```

Merged demo profile:

```bash
docker compose --profile merged up --build merged-demo
```

## See the docs (Mintlify)

Schema MDX is generated before dev:

```bash
pnpm gen:docs
pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev
```

`predev` on the docs package also runs the schema generator, so `pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev` alone is enough if you use that script. Mintlify prints the local URL in the terminal (often **http://localhost:3000**, which conflicts with Next.js web—run docs and web in different terminals or stop one first).

## See `@leash/core` (library, not a separate UI)

- **Code**: `packages/core/src/` (entry is `src/index.ts`).
- **Docs in Mintlify**: after `pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev`, open the **SDK → @leash/core** page (`apps/docs/sdk/core.mdx`).
- **Package README**: `packages/core/README.md`.
- **Behavior in tests**: `pnpm --filter @leash/core test` (runs Vitest for that package).

## See the demo UI (Next.js “web” explorer)

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/runner start
LEASH_RUNNER_URL=http://localhost:8787 pnpm --filter @leash/web dev
```

Then open **http://localhost:3000** — home, `/explore`, `/new`, and `/a/[mint]` (receipt JSONL is proxied from the runner when `LEASH_RUNNER_URL` points at it).

**Quick scripted buyer→seller ping** (needs seller up):

```bash
SELLER_URL=http://localhost:3001 pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e-demo.ts
```

Checking `docs.json`: Mintlify rejects `colors.background` — we'll fix the config to match Mintlify's schema.

[REDACTED]

Mintlify's `colors` object only accepts `primary`, `light`, and `dark` (hex strings). Nesting `background` under `colors` is invalid — page background belongs under the top-level `background.color` object.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

## Why you saw that error

Mintlify validates `docs.json` against its schema. Under **`colors`**, only these keys are allowed (all hex strings):

- `primary` (required)
- `light`
- `dark`

Your file had:

```json
"colors": {
  ...
  "background": { "dark": "#0b0b0f" }
}
```

`background` is **not** a valid child of `colors`, so the CLI reported:

`#.colors: Unrecognized key(s) in object: 'background'`

## Fix

1. **Removed** `background` from inside `colors`.
2. **Moved** the near-black canvas color to the correct place: top-level **`background.color.dark`** (and **`background.color.light`** for light mode), which [Mintlify’s docs](https://mintlify.com/docs/organize/settings-reference) describe for page background.

`colors.dark` is meant for UI accents (e.g. buttons), not the full page fill, so `colors.dark` was set to a dark purple (`#1a1533`) while **`#0b0b0f`** stays on **`background.color.dark`**.

After pulling the updated `apps/docs/docs.json`, run again:

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev
```

You should get past the prebuild step; if anything else fails, paste the new terminal output.

Adding `style.css` using Mintlify’s documented selectors (`nav-tabs`, `topbar-right-container`, `#search-bar-entry`) to swap the tab strip and search cluster via flex `order`.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Improving compatibility: Mintlify documents `navigation-items` as the tab container in some layouts.

[REDACTED]

Here is what was going on and what we did.

## Why there is no `docs.json` switch

Mintlify does not expose a setting to reorder the top bar (tabs vs search). Those pieces are fixed by the theme, so you change layout with **custom CSS**, which Mintlify documents under [Custom scripts](https://mintlify.com/docs/customize/custom-scripts) (stable hooks like `#navbar`, `nav-tabs`, `topbar-right-container`, `#search-bar-entry`).

## What we added

A new file **`apps/docs/style.css`** next to `docs.json`. Mintlify picks up `.css` files in the docs content root and applies them globally (same pattern as their `#navbar { … }` example).

The layout logic:

1. Make **`#navbar`** a flex row (with `!important` so it wins over theme defaults if needed).
2. **`nav-logo`** — `order: 1` so the logo stays first.
3. **`topbar-right-container`** — `order: 2` so the **search + theme/assistant** cluster sits in the **middle-left** (where the tab strip usually lives).
4. **`navigation-items` / `nav-tabs`** — `order: 3` and **`margin-left: auto`** so the **Documentation** tab strip sits on the **right** (where search used to be).

So visually you get: **logo → search area → (flex space) → tabs**, instead of **logo → tabs → … → search**.

## What you should do

Restart the docs dev server:

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev
```

If anything looks off after a Mintlify upgrade, use **Inspect** on `#navbar` and tweak `order` on the flex children that actually wrap `nav-tabs` vs `#search-bar-entry` / `topbar-right-container`—Mintlify warns that DOM details can change over time.

- **`mdx-content`**: **`min(90rem, 100%)`** so the MDX body (and code inside it) can use that width.
- **`code-block` / `code-group`**: **`width: 100%`** and **`max-width: 100%`** so fences and tabbed code use the full MDX width; **`pre`** stays **`max-width: 100%`** with horizontal scroll for long lines.

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

## Why prev/next looked different from the sidebar

Mintlify’s footer links are not just the sidebar label. They often add:

1. An **`eyebrow`** line (group / section name, like “SDK reference”).
2. A second line from frontmatter **`description`** (meant for SEO, but reused in nav-style cards).
3. For API pages, a **`method-pill`** (GET/POST, etc.).

The **sidebar** usually shows only the short label (`title`, or `sidebarTitle` when you set it).

## What we did

In **`apps/docs/style.css`** we added rules that, **only in the bottom pagination area**, hide:

- **`eyebrow`** (section label),
- **`method-pill`**,
- **Direct `p` children** of `pagination-prev` / `pagination-next` (the usual place for the `description` line),
- **`nav-dropdown-item-description`** when it appears **inside** those pagination links (some layouts reuse that markup).

So the footer should read much closer to “title only,” like the sidebar.


## If the **wording** still doesn’t match the sidebar

CSS can’t rename text. Mintlify’s footer still uses the page **`title`** for that line, while the sidebar can use **`sidebarTitle`**. If those differ, either:

- Shorten **`title`** so it matches what you want in both places.

Searching Mintlify docs and the project for what controls prev/next pagination.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is how Mintlify decides what shows in the **previous / next** footer. Nothing in your repo is “the pagination component” — that UI is built into Mintlify’s app; you only control **inputs** and **styling**.

### 1. **Order of pages → which page is “next”**

That comes entirely from **`docs.json` → `navigation`**.

- Pages are ordered by how they appear in **`tabs` → `groups` → `pages`** (flattened in list order).
- “Next” is the following entry in that navigation tree (within the same tab / scope Mintlify uses for that section).

So the **“which page”** part is your **`apps/docs/docs.json`** navigation arrays, not a separate “pagination” setting.

### 2. **Title and description text on the cards**

That comes from **each page’s MDX frontmatter** at the top of the file.

Mintlify’s own [Pages](https://mintlify.com/docs/organize/pages) docs say:

- **`title`** — used for the page title (navigation, browser tab, etc.).
- **`description`** — “A brief description of what this page covers. **Displays under the title** and improves SEO.”

That “under the title” behavior is the same metadata the product reuses in places like **prev/next cards**, so the **subtitle you see before “Next”** is almost always the target page’s **`description`** (or similar derived copy if the theme fills it in).

So the **“what text”** part is mainly:

- **`title`** in `introduction.mdx`, `quickstart.mdx`, etc.
- **`description`** in those same files (remove or shorten it if you don’t want a subtitle anywhere Mintlify shows it).

### 3. **The actual UI (buttons, layout)**

That is **Mintlify’s closed-source theme/renderer** (e.g. web components like `pagination-next` in the DOM). You do **not** have a source file for that in the monorepo; the only levers are:

- **Frontmatter + `docs.json`** (content and order), or  
- **`style.css` / custom scripts** (hide or restyle what they render).

**Summary:** **`docs.json`** picks **which** page is next; **MDX frontmatter** (`title`, especially **`description`**) supplies **most of the copy** on the card; **Mintlify’s app** draws the footer. To show **only the title** in a first-class way, the cleanest fix is usually to **drop `description`** from pages where you don’t want that line (SEO can still use other fields if Mintlify supports them in your plan, or you accept shorter descriptions). CSS is a fallback when you still want `description` for SEO but not in pagination.

[REDACTED]

Here is what that guide is describing, tied to what the code actually does.

### What “merged agent” means

Normally you might run **seller** and **buyer** as separate apps (or processes). A **merged** agent is **one process** that is both:

1. **Seller** — exposes paid HTTP routes (here `POST /echo` with an x402-style gate from `createSeller`).
2. **Buyer** — periodically calls that same server (or another seller) using `createBuyer`, obeying **rules** (budget, allowed hosts, interval trigger).

They share the **same on-chain identity** for the demo: one `AGENT_ASSET` mint string is passed into both `createSeller` and `createBuyer` as the agent. So it is “one logical agent” doing earn and spend in one place.

### What the doc page tells you to do

The MDX file only says:

- The demo lives in `apps/merged-demo`.
- Start it with Docker Compose using the **`merged`** profile so the `merged-demo` service builds and runs.
- In words: one Hono app runs `createSeller`, and an interval-driven `createBuyer` hits the same `AGENT_ASSET`; **earn** and **spend** receipts are supposed to share **one nonce sequence** on that feed (that is the “merged” receipt story at a high level).

### What happens in `apps/merged-demo/src/index.ts`

1. **Umi + RPC** — Connects to Solana (default devnet RPC) and loads mpl-core for seller-kit.
2. **`createSeller(app, …)`** — Registers the x402 gate for `POST /echo` and wires payment metadata; the route handler itself is trivial (`{ echo: true }`).
3. **`createBuyer({ agent: asset, rules: … })`** — Builds a buyer that uses the **same** `asset` as the seller’s `sellerAgent.asset`.
4. **`setInterval` every 20s** — Calls `buyer.fetch('http://127.0.0.1:${port}/echo', { method: 'POST' })`, so the **buyer is calling the seller on the same machine and port** the app just opened — a tight loop for demos.
5. **`serve`** — One server on `PORT` (default 3003).

So in one sentence: **the merged-demo is a single Node server that charges for `/echo` as a seller and also, on a timer, pays to call `/echo` as a buyer, both keyed to the same agent asset**, which is what the short guide is pointing you at when it mentions Docker and shared receipts.

Here is the short answer, and the guide is updated so readers without Docker are not stuck.

### If they do not have Docker

The merged demo is just **`node`** running the compiled app in `apps/merged-demo`. They need:

1. **Node ≥ 20** and **pnpm**  
2. From the **`leash/`** directory: install deps, build the app (and its workspace deps), then start:

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm turbo run build --filter=@leash/merged-demo
pnpm --filter @leash/merged-demo start
```

`turbo`’s `build` task uses `dependsOn: ["^build"]`, so building `@leash/merged-demo` also builds packages it depends on (e.g. seller-kit, buyer-kit) before producing `dist/`.

They can set **`PORT`**, **`SOLANA_RPC`**, and **`AGENT_ASSET`** the same way as in `docker-compose.yml` if they need non-defaults.

Docker is only a convenience wrapper around that same process; it is not required for the merged agent behavior itself.

The **Build a merged agent** doc now has **With Docker** and **Without Docker** sections plus a note to run Compose from `leash/`.

Checking MDX structure: the right column is usually the in-page Table of Contents, which only lists headings from the current page.

[REDACTED]

Here is what is going on.

### What that right column is

In Mintlify (especially the **maple** theme), the column on the **right** is usually **“On this page”** — a **table of contents for the current MDX file only**. It is built from **headings in that page** (`#` / `##` / `###`), not from `docs.json` groups like “Concepts” or “Guides”.

So it is **not** a “group tab” for the whole section. It only reflects **this page’s outline**.

### Why “Getting started” looks full and others look empty

Your **Getting started** pages actually define several `##` sections, so the TOC has something to list:

- `introduction.mdx` has `## Monorepo`.
- `quickstart.mdx` has `## Prerequisites`, `## Install + build`, `## Run the split stack`, etc.

Many other pages are **short stubs with no `##` (or `###`) after the intro**, so there is **nothing to put in that TOC**. For example:

- `concepts/agent.mdx` is only body text after the frontmatter — **no `##` headings**.
- `guides/build-a-seller.mdx` jumps straight into a code block — **no `##` headings**.

So the right rail is still there, but it has **no entries** (or Mintlify hides/minimizes it when empty), which feels like “it doesn’t show for Concepts / Guides / …”.

### Summary

| Left sidebar | Right “On this page” |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Comes from **`docs.json`** navigation (groups + pages) | Comes from **headings inside the current `.mdx`** |

To get a similar right-hand outline on Concepts / Guides / SDK pages, add normal markdown structure, e.g. `## Overview`, `## See also`, `### Step 1`, etc., in those MDX files.

Here is how to run **`@leash/web`** (`apps/web`).

### Dev (hot reload)

From the **`leash/`** monorepo root (where `pnpm-workspace.yaml` is):

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm --filter @leash/web dev
```

That runs **`next dev -p 3000`**, so open **http://localhost:3000**.

The README says the explorer proxies JSONL to the runner. If you use that flow, start the runner (or point at one) and set:

```bash
export LEASH_RUNNER_URL=http://localhost:8787
pnpm --filter @leash/web dev
```

Default is already `http://localhost:8787` if unset.

### Production-style

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/web build
pnpm --filter @leash/web start
```

Again **port 3000** unless you change the scripts in `apps/web/package.json`.

- `components/wallet-button.tsx` — Privy login + Solana embedded wallet, copyable address.

**Pages**

| Route | Drives |
|------|--------|
| `/` | Dashboard with kill-switch state, stat cards, quick links, 1-2-3 onboarding strip. |
| `/runner` | Live tail of any agent's `receipts.jsonl` with adjustable poll interval. |
| `/agents` | Local store of tracked Core asset mints + capability tag, opens profile. |
| `/agents/[mint]` | Asset Signer PDA (computed via `resolveSellerPayTo`), earn/spend counts, receipt feed, registration document resolver tab. |
| `/seller` | Toggles `x-payment` header against the built-in echo gate so you can watch the 402 vs allow path. |
| `/buyer` | Form for `RulesV1` (budget, hosts, interval), fires `createBuyer().fetch()` server-side, renders the response + the receipt + a session history list. |
| `/schemas` | Live validator for `ReceiptV1` / `RulesV1` / `RegistrationV1` / `LeashBlockV1` with templates. |

**API proxies** (so the browser never bundles Node-only SDKs)
- `GET /api/runner/health` and `/api/runner/pause` → `@leash/runner`
- `GET /api/receipts/[mint]` → parses NDJSON with `@leash/schemas`
- `POST /api/buyer/fire` → `@leash/buyer-kit` `createBuyer().fetch`
- `POST /api/seller/echo` → mirrors `@leash/seller-kit` `simpleX402Gate`
- `GET /api/seller/payTo?asset=…` → `@leash/seller-kit` `resolveSellerPayTo`
- `GET /api/registry/resolve?uri=…` → `@leash/registry-utils` `resolveByoUri`
- `POST /api/schemas/validate` → live Zod schemas

**Old pages removed**: `app/new`, `app/explore`, `app/a/[mint]/page.tsx` (the JSONL route under `app/a/[mint]/receipts.jsonl/route.ts` is preserved for compatibility).

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/runner start                    # so /api/runner/* turns green
```

Here is what **`EADDRINUSE`** means and what we changed.

### Cause
Something is already bound to **port 8787** (often another `leash-runner`, Docker’s `runner` service, or a leftover Node process). Only one process can listen on that port.

On your machine it was **`node` PID 91721** (likely a runner left running from an earlier start).

### What to do

**Option A — Stop the existing listener**

```bash
lsof -nP -iTCP:8787 -sTCP:LISTEN
kill <PID>
pnpm --filter @leash/runner start
```

**Option B — Use another port**

```bash
PORT=8788 pnpm --filter @leash/runner start
```

Then set **`LEASH_RUNNER_URL=http://localhost:8788`** in `apps/web/.env` so the playground still talks to the right URL.

### Code change
`packages/runner/src/cli.ts` now attaches an **`error`** handler on the HTTP server. If the port is in use, it prints those hints and exits with code **1** instead of an unhandled `Error: listen EADDRINUSE` stack trace. `packages/runner/README.md` documents the same.

Rebuild once so `dist/cli.js` includes it: `pnpm --filter @leash/runner build` (or your usual turbo build).

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Root cause

In `apps/web/.env` you set:

`NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_CLIENT_ID=privy_app_secret_…`

That value is your **Privy app secret**, not an **app client id**. Passing it as `clientId` on `PrivyProvider` is invalid and typically breaks login (including email OTP).

Using **`NEXT_PUBLIC_`** for a secret is also wrong: anything with that prefix is embedded in the client bundle, so the secret is exposed to anyone who loads the site. You should **remove that line from `.env`**, restart `next dev`, and **rotate the app secret** in the Privy dashboard (treat it as compromised).

### What you need in env

- **`NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_APP_ID`** — your real app id (from the dashboard). That is enough for the React SDK in most setups.
- **`NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_CLIENT_ID`** — optional; only if you created an **app client** under *Configuration → App settings → Clients*. It must **not** be `privy_app_secret_…`.

### Code change (already done)

- `getPrivyClientId()` in `lib/env.ts` returns `undefined` if the value looks like `privy_app_secret_*`, so we never pass a secret as `clientId`.
- `PrivyProvider` only receives `clientId` when a safe value exists.
- `.env.local.example` and `apps/web/README.md` document this.

After you delete the bad `NEXT_PUBLIC_PRIVY_CLIENT_ID` line (or clear it), restart the dev server and try email again. If it still fails, in the Privy dashboard enable **Email** as a login method and add **`http://localhost:3000`** (or your dev URL) under **allowed origins**.

Here is a concise picture of how things are organized and what that workflow file is for.

### What `experimentals/.github/workflows/leash-ci.yml` is

It is a **GitHub Actions** definition: on **push** or **pull request** to `main`, when files under **`leash/**`** change, GitHub runs a job that:

1. Checks out the repo  
2. Installs **pnpm 9** and **Node 20**  
3. `cd`s into **`leash/`** (`working-directory: leash`)  
4. Runs **`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`**, then **`typecheck`**, **`lint`**, **`test`**, **`build`**

So it is **continuous integration (CI)** for the Leash monorepo: it catches broken TypeScript, lint, tests, or builds before or after merge.

It only makes sense if the **Git repository root** is something like **`experimentals/`** and **`leash/`** is a subfolder. Comments were added at the top of that file to spell that out.

### What to push for `leash/`

Your **`leash/.gitignore`** already keeps the right things out: **`node_modules`**, **`dist`**, **`.next`**, **`.env`**, **`.env.local`**, logs, etc. You should commit:

- **`pnpm-lock.yaml`**, **`package.json`**, **`pnpm-workspace.yaml`**, **`turbo.json`**, ts/eslint config  
- All **`packages/**`**, **`apps/**`**, **`scripts/**`**, **`docker/**`**, docs, **`README.md`**, **`CONTRIBUTING.md`**, **`LEASH.md`**  
- **`.github/workflows/ci.yml`** (new, see below)  
- **`apps/web/.env.local.example`** (template only — no secrets)

Do **not** commit **`apps/web/.env`** if it contains secrets (Privy app secret, etc.). `.env` is ignored, but if you ever force-add it, rotate any exposed keys.

### If GitHub’s repo root **is** `leash/` (not `experimentals/`)

The file **`experimentals/.github/workflows/leash-ci.yml` will not exist** in that repo, so CI would not run unless you add a workflow **inside** `leash/`.

A second workflow was added:

**`leash/.github/workflows/ci.yml`**

Same steps as above, but with **no** `working-directory: leash` (the checkout root is already the monorepo). It runs on push/PR to **`main`** or **`master`**.

The **`leash/README.md`** “GitHub: what to push” section documents both layouts and points to these two workflow files.

Fixing the workflow: `pnpm/action-setup` v4 conflicts when both `version:` and `packageManager` specify pnpm. Aligning both workflow files with `package.json`.

[REDACTED]

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Why GitHub failed

**`pnpm/action-setup@v4`** reads **two** places for the pnpm version:

1. The workflow: `with: { version: 9 }`
2. Root **`package.json`**: `"packageManager": "pnpm@9.15.0"`

Those disagree (`9` vs `9.15.0`), so the action **refuses to run** and prints the “Multiple versions of pnpm specified” error.

### Fix

In **`leash/.github/workflows/ci.yml`** the explicit `version: 9` was **removed**. The action will use the **`packageManager`** field in `package.json` (`pnpm@9.15.0`) as the single source of truth.

Push again and the workflow should get past the pnpm setup step.

### If you have another workflow (e.g. parent repo)

Apply the same rule: **either** only `packageManager` in `package.json` **or** only `version:` in the action, **not both**. Prefer dropping `version:` and relying on `packageManager`.

Here is what was tuned so CI is more reliable on GitHub and locally.

### `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- **`concurrency`** — new pushes cancel older runs on the same branch.
- **`permissions: contents: read`** — least privilege.
- **`HUSKY: '0'`** — skips Husky during `pnpm install` so hooks never break CI.
- **`NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED`** / **`TURBO_TELEMETRY_DISABLED`** — quieter, slightly faster.
- **`cache-dependency-path: pnpm-lock.yaml`** — makes pnpm cache for `setup-node` reliable.
- **Single step `pnpm run ci`** — one definition shared with local (less drift than five separate steps).

### `turbo.json`
- Build **`outputs`** include **`schemas/**`** (paths are **per package**). That matches `@leash/docs`’s generated `apps/docs/schemas/*.mdx` and removes the Turbo warning about `@leash/docs#build` having no outputs.

### `package.json` `ci` script
- Now runs **`pnpm format:check`** first, then typecheck → lint → test → build, aligned with the workflow.

### Formatting
- Ran **`pnpm format`** so the repo matches Prettier; **`pnpm format:check`** passes.

### Local note
- If **`next build`** fails on **`copyfile`** under `.next/standalone`, wipe **`apps/web/.next`** and build again (fresh GitHub runners usually do not hit this).

Push these changes and re-run the workflow; it should get past pnpm setup and match what **`HUSKY=0 pnpm run ci`** does locally.

### Why it failed

`tests/cli.test.ts` runs the real CLI with:

`node …/packages/testing/dist/cli/conformance.js`

That file is produced by **`pnpm --filter @leash/testing build`** (`tsc` → `dist/cli/conformance.js`).

Your **`ci`** script was:

`… && pnpm test && pnpm build`

So on GitHub, **tests ran before any `turbo build`**, `dist/` was still missing → **MODULE_NOT_FOUND**.

`vitest` also does not go through Turbo’s graph when you only look at `pnpm test` from a filter, so nothing built the package first unless Turbo ordered it.

### What we changed

1. **`package.json` `ci`** — order is now **`… && pnpm build && pnpm test`** so a full monorepo build runs before tests (same idea as “compile, then verify”).

2. **`turbo.json`** — `"test"` now has **`dependsOn": ["^build", "build"]`**, so `pnpm turbo run test` always runs **that package’s `build`** before its tests (CLI exists even if someone runs tests without a separate build step).

3. **`packages/testing/package.json`** — added **`"pretest": "pnpm run build"`** so **`pnpm --filter @leash/testing test`** still works when run **without** Turbo.

After you push, the `@leash/testing` job should pass.

I need to walk through each package systematically and explain what capabilities they unlock for the user.

Here's a complete tour of `leash/packages/` — what each module actually does today, what's stubbed, and what the playground is and isn't showing.

---

## 1. The package map

```
@leash/schemas         pure types + Zod parsers (no runtime logic)
@leash/core            policy gate, receipts, x402, kill-switch, treasury (pure TS)
@leash/registry-utils  ERC-8004 registration + Pinata + on-chain identity link
@leash/seller-kit      Hono middleware to gate routes with x402 + derive payTo PDA
@leash/buyer-kit       outbound HTTP client wired to policy + receipts
@leash/runner          long-running HTTP service: /health, /pause, receipts feed
@leash/testing         mocks (x402 server, facilitator) + JSONL conformance CLI
```

They form a layered stack: `schemas → core → (seller-kit | buyer-kit | registry-utils) → runner → apps`.

---

## 2. `@leash/schemas` — the source of truth

Four Zod schemas, nothing else:

- **`RulesV1`** (`rules.ts`) — what an agent is allowed to do: `budget.daily`, `budget.perCall`, `currency: USDC`, `hosts.allow/deny`, `priceCeiling`, `triggers` (cron / webhook / interval), `stopOn`.
- **`ReceiptV1`** (`receipt.ts`) — one row per outbound call: `kind: spend|earn`, `agent`, `nonce`, `decision: allow|deny`, `price`, `tx_sig`, `prev_receipt_hash`, `receipt_hash`. This is the JSONL line shape.
- **`RegistrationV1`** (`registration.ts`) — ERC-8004-shaped JSON with optional `services[]` and an optional `leash` block.
- **`LeashBlockV1`** (`leash-block.ts`) — the `leash` sub-document: `rulesUri` (where rules live), `receiptsFeed` (where the JSONL feed lives), optional `anchor`, optional `killSwitch`.
- **`networks.ts`** — CAIP-2 ids (`solana:101`, `solana:103`).
- **`capabilities.ts`** — `inferCapabilities(doc)` → `('buyer' | 'seller')[]` from a registration doc.

Everything else in the monorepo consumes these.

---

## 3. `@leash/core` — the engine (pure TS, no chain calls)

```9:13:leash/packages/core/src/index.ts
export * from './x402/client.js';
export * from './x402/parse.js';
export * from './treasury/balance.js';
export * from './treasury/withdraw.js';
export * from './treasury/pause.js';
export * from './wallet/index.js';
```

### `agent/agent.ts` — the only "Agent" abstraction

```5:17:leash/packages/core/src/agent/agent.ts
export class Agent {
  readonly asset: PublicKey;

  constructor(asset: string | PublicKey) {
    this.asset = typeof asset === 'string' ? publicKey(asset) : asset;
  }

  /** Asset Signer PDA (treasury) — requires Umi context for PDA derivation. */
  treasuryPda(umi: Pick<Context, 'eddsa' | 'programs'>): PublicKey {
    const [address] = findAssetSignerPda(umi, { asset: this.asset });
    return address;
  }
}
```

This is critical to understand: **`Agent` is just a wrapper around an existing MPL Core asset mint**. It does NOT mint the asset. There is no `createAgent()` anywhere in the monorepo. The expected flow is:

1. The user calls Umi + `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core` directly to mint a Core asset (the "agent NFT").
2. They take that asset's pubkey and pass it into `new Agent(asset)`.
3. The "treasury" of that agent is the **Asset Signer PDA** — a deterministic PDA owned by the asset itself. Funds sent to that PDA are spendable only via mpl-core `Execute`.

So when you ask "where do I create an agent in the playground" — the answer is: **that step is intentionally outside `@leash/core`**, because Leash is a policy/receipts layer on top of an asset you already minted. The README/docs guide users to mint the Core asset themselves.

### `policy/evaluate.ts` — the gate

A pure function `evaluate(req, rules, state) → { decision: 'allow' | 'deny', reason? }`. Checks in order:

1. Replay (request hash already seen)
2. `hosts.deny` match
3. `hosts.allow` non-match
4. `priceCeiling`
5. Daily budget (already over OR would push over after this call)
6. Per-call max

Zero IO. Tested as a pure function.

### `receipt/{hash,build,verify}.ts` — the audit log

- **`requestHash({method,url,body,headers})`** → `sha256:<hex>` over a canonicalized request.
- **`canonicalJson(value)`** → stable JSON with sorted keys, used to hash receipts deterministically.
- **`finalizeReceipt(draft)`** → adds `receipt_hash` (sha256 of the canonicalized draft).
- **`verifyReceiptChain(lines)`** → walks a JSONL feed and checks: schema valid, gapless `nonce` (0,1,2,…), `prev_receipt_hash` chains, and recomputed `receipt_hash` matches. This is what gives Leash its "tamper-evident log" property.

### `x402/client.ts` — `x402Fetch`

Thin wrapper around `fetch`. If the server returns 402, calls `onPaymentRequired()` for headers (typically `x-payment`), retries once, returns `{request, status, txSig, response}`. `txSig` is read straight from the response header `x-tx-sig`.

`x402/parse.ts` is a **stub** — `parse402Body` returns `{ raw: '' }`. Real x402 challenge parsing is the "follow-up PR" comment.

### `treasury/`

- **`balance.ts`** — `getSplTokenBalance(connection, owner, mint)` using `@solana/web3.js`. Real, working — sums all token accounts for an owner under a given SPL mint. You'd call this with `owner = agent.treasuryPda(umi)` to read the agent's USDC balance.
- **`withdraw.ts`** — explicit **stub**. `describeWithdraw()` just returns a string telling you to compose `mpl-core Execute` + SPL `transferChecked` yourself. No code does the withdraw today.
- **`pause.ts`** — `createPauseResolver({ fetchOnchainPaused, cacheTtlMs })` returns a function that returns `{ paused, source: 'env'|'onchain'|'cache' }`. Honors `LEASH_KILL=1` (env hard stop), then calls your on-chain reader with a 5s cache, fail-open on RPC error. **It does not itself read the AppData plugin** — you wire that in via `fetchOnchainPaused`. The default in the runner just mirrors `LEASH_ONCHAIN_PAUSED=1`.

### `wallet/index.ts` — one line

`export type Wallet = Signer;` (a Umi `Signer`). No wallet creation, no key management. The host app supplies a Umi signer.

---

## 4. `@leash/registry-utils` — ERC-8004 + IPFS + on-chain link

This is the only package that actually writes on-chain.

- **`pinata.ts` → `pinJson(json, {jwt})`** — POSTs to `api.pinata.cloud/pinning/pinJSONToIPFS` with `PINATA_JWT`, returns `{cid, uri: 'ipfs://…', gatewayUrl}`.
- **`verify-cid.ts` → `verifyGatewayCid(url)`** — sanity-checks the gateway returns 200.
- **`resolve.ts`**:
  - `resolveByoUri(uri)` — fetches a registration doc and validates it as `RegistrationV1`.
  - `resolveOrUpload({uri | json})` — either resolves an existing URI or pins a JSON doc to Pinata and validates the gateway.
- **`read.ts` → `loadRegistrationDocument(uri)`** — same as `resolveByoUri` but throws typed errors.
- **`leash-block.ts` → `parseLeashBlock(input)`** — Zod parse helper.
- **`register-identity.ts` → `registerAgentIdentity(umi, {asset, collection, agentRegistrationUri})`** — calls `mpl-agent-registry`'s `registerIdentityV1` and confirms the tx. **This is the only function in the whole monorepo that actually writes the agent's identity URI on-chain.**

---

## 5. `@leash/seller-kit` — earn side

- **`seller/agent-seller.ts` → `resolveSellerPayTo(umi, {asset})`** — derives the Asset Signer PDA (the same PDA `Agent.treasuryPda` returns) and returns it as a string. This is the address payments should be sent to.
- **`hono/simple-x402.ts` → `simpleX402Gate()`** — Hono middleware: returns 402 if `x-payment` header is missing, otherwise `next()`. The note says "swap for `@x402/hono` + PayAI facilitator in production".
- **`hono/create-seller.ts` → `createSeller(app, {umi, sellerAgent, routes})`** — registers the gate on each `"METHOD /path"` key and returns `{ payTo }`.
- **`receipts/store.ts`** — just an interface (`ReceiptStore { append }`). No implementation here; the seller demo does its own appending.

So a seller is an HTTP server that (a) charges 402 without a payment header and (b) tells the world to send funds to `payTo`, the agent's PDA.

---

## 6. `@leash/buyer-kit` — spend side

`createBuyer({ agent, rules, spentToday? }) → { fetch(url, init) }`. Each `fetch` call:

1. Hashes the request.
2. Runs `evaluate()` from `@leash/core`. If denied, builds a `deny` receipt, returns a synthetic 403.
3. Otherwise calls `x402Fetch` with `onPaymentRequired: () => ({'x-payment': 'mock'})` — yes, today the payment header is the literal string `'mock'`. Real payment construction is the next PR.
4. Builds an `allow` receipt with `facilitator: 'local'`, the captured `tx_sig` (whatever the seller put in `x-tx-sig`), then finalizes with `receipt_hash`.
5. Returns `{ response, receipt }` to the caller.

The buyer does **not** persist receipts itself — the host app decides what to do with them (post to runner, write to disk, anchor, etc.). It also doesn't update `spentToday` from the actual price — `recentRequestHashes` grows but the budget arithmetic is a known v0.1 gap.

---

## 7. `@leash/runner` — the always-on service

A tiny Hono app:

```25:43:leash/packages/runner/src/http/server.ts
app.get('/health', async (c) => {
  const state = await resolvePause();
  return c.json({ ok: !state.paused, paused: state.paused, source: state.source });
});
app.get('/pause', async (c) => { ... });
app.get('/a/:mint/receipts.jsonl', (c) => {
  const mint = c.req.param('mint');
  const lines = listLines(store, mint);
  return new Response(lines.join('\n') + (lines.length ? '\n' : ''), {
    headers: { 'content-type': 'application/x-ndjson' },
  });
});
```

Storage is `Map<mint, string[]>` — **in-memory only, cleared on restart**. The runner's CLI (`cli.ts`) just spins this up on `PORT=8787`. There is **no `POST` to ingest receipts and no DB**. The way receipts get there in v0.1 is whoever has the `ReceiptStore` calls `appendLine()` directly — i.e., the runner has to be the same process as the buyer/seller, or you swap `createMemoryStore()` for a shared store.

That's why the playground shows "0 receipts" for any mint you type in — nothing is writing to the runner from the web app.

---

## 8. `@leash/testing` — mocks + conformance

- **`mocks/x402-server.ts`** — Hono app with `/ping` and `/tag` that return 402 unless `x-payment` is present. Lets buyers be tested end-to-end without a real seller.
- **`mocks/facilitator.ts`** — `LocalFacilitator.verify()` always says valid; `settle()` returns `mock-<hex>` and remembers the payment.
- **`fixtures/receipts.ts`** — canned receipt sequences.
- **`conformance/receipt-feed.ts` + `cli/conformance.ts`** — `leash-conformance <feed.jsonl>` exits 0 iff the feed passes `verifyReceiptChain`. This is the "is your agent feed honest?" bar.

---

## 9. So what's actually possible today, end-to-end?

| Capability | Status |
|---|---|
| Define rules, validate them | ✅ `@leash/schemas` |
| Evaluate a request against rules | ✅ `evaluate()` |
| Build a tamper-evident receipt | ✅ `finalizeReceipt`, `verifyReceiptChain` |
| Buyer that gates+records calls | ✅ `createBuyer` (with `x-payment: 'mock'` placeholder) |
| Seller that 402s without payment | ✅ `createSeller` + `simpleX402Gate` |
| Resolve seller `payTo` (Asset Signer PDA) | ✅ `resolveSellerPayTo` |
| Read agent USDC balance | ✅ `getSplTokenBalance` |
| **Withdraw from agent treasury** | ❌ stub only — must compose `mpl-core Execute` + SPL transfer yourself |
| Real x402 payment construction / verification | ❌ `x402/parse.ts` is empty; buyer sends literal `'mock'` |
| Pause/kill switch | ⚠️ env breaker (`LEASH_KILL`) works today; on-chain AppData read needs a `fetchOnchainPaused` you supply |
| **Mint a new agent** (Core asset) | ❌ not in the monorepo at all — use Umi + `mpl-core` directly |
| Pin registration to IPFS | ✅ `pinJson` (needs `PINATA_JWT`) |
| Link registration on-chain | ✅ `registerAgentIdentity` |
| **Enumerate agents created by a wallet** | ❌ no indexer; chain query not implemented |
| Persistent receipt storage | ❌ runner uses in-memory `Map`, lost on restart |
| Anchor receipts to a Merkle account | ❌ schema slot exists in `LeashBlockV1.anchor`, no implementation |

---

## 10. Why the playground doesn't show a "Create Agent" button

The playground is faithful to what the SDK exposes. Specifically:

- There's no `core.createAgent()` to call, so there's no UI for it. The Agents page lets you **paste an existing mint** and label it; that's the v0.1 contract.
- There's no `runner.postReceipt()` endpoint, so the buyer playground produces a receipt object on-screen but doesn't push it into the runner's feed — which is why `/a/<mint>/receipts.jsonl` is always empty unless something else is appending.
- There's no `getAgentsForOwner()`, so we can't list "your agents" — the agents list is local-storage only.
- There's no `withdraw()`, so the agent profile shows the PDA (`payTo`) and earn/spend counts but can't sweep funds.

---

## 11. What we could realistically add to the playground without changing the SDK contract

If you want the playground to feel "complete" against the v0.1 surface, these are concrete, no-new-protocol additions:

1. **Mint Agent (Core asset)** — direct `mpl-core` `create()` call from the browser using Privy's embedded wallet as the Umi signer. Then auto-call `registerAgentIdentity` with a registration JSON the user fills in (or pin via `pinJson`).
2. **Treasury balance** — call `getSplTokenBalance(connection, agent.treasuryPda(umi), USDC_MINT)` on the agent profile page; show USDC balance next to the PDA.

Looking at the Metaplex agents documentation and the markdown file they've shared to understand what they're asking for.

I'm looking at Metaplex's Agent Registry documentation and realizing that `mintAndSubmitAgent` from the mpl-agent-registry package is the solution I was looking for — it handles both creating the Core asset and registering the Agent Identity PDA in a single transaction, which closes the gap in the agent creation flow.

The user is suggesting we implement this using the ERC-8004 / Metaplex 104 standard to add a Create Agent feature to the playground. Let me check what version of mpl-agent-registry we currently have installed.

Yes — exactly. This is Metaplex's **MIP-104 / Agent Identity** flow, and you're already on `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry@0.2.5`, which exposes `mintAgent` / `mintAndSubmitAgent`. That's the missing "Create Agent" primitive.

Quick recap of how it slots into the Leash stack:

## What `mintAndSubmitAgent` does

One atomic tx that:

1. Creates an MPL Core asset (the agent NFT, owned by the wallet you pass).
2. Attaches the **Agent Identity plugin** to it, with lifecycle Listen hooks on `Transfer` / `Update` / `Execute`.
3. Stores `agentMetadata` (services, registrations, supportedTrust, …) **off-chain via `https://api.metaplex.com`**.
4. Stores `uri` **on-chain** as the asset's standard NFT metadata pointer.

Returns `{ assetAddress, signature }`. That `assetAddress` is exactly what we feed into `new Agent(assetAddress)` in `@leash/core`, and into `findAssetSignerPda` to derive the agent's treasury PDA.

## How it relates to what we already have

| Piece | Current Leash code | Metaplex API does it for you |
|---|---|---|
| Mint Core asset | not implemented | ✅ |
| Attach Agent Identity plugin | `registerAgentIdentity()` (post-hoc) | ✅ in same tx |
| Pin metadata to IPFS | `pinJson()` (Pinata) | ✅ off-chain via api.metaplex.com |
| Pre-built `RegistrationV1` schema | `@leash/schemas/RegistrationV1` (ERC-8004 shape) | mostly compatible — Metaplex's `agentMetadata` adds `type: 'agent'` and drops `image` |

So the two flows we now support cleanly are:

- **One-shot (Metaplex API)**: `mintAndSubmitAgent` — easiest, what the playground should default to.
- **BYO (existing)**: pin your own JSON to Pinata, mint your own Core asset, then call `registerAgentIdentity` — what we have today.
