# Register an Agent

Last updated March 12, 2026

Ask AI

Skill file available

CLI available

Register an agent on the Metaplex 014 agent registry by binding an identity record to an MPL Core asset. 

## Summary

The `registerIdentityV1` instruction binds an on-chain identity record to an MPL Core asset, creating a discoverable PDA and attaching lifecycle hooks for Transfer, Update, and Execute.

* **Creates** a PDA derived from the asset's public key for on-chain discoverability
* **Attaches** an `AgentIdentity` plugin with lifecycle hooks to the Core asset
* **Links** to an off-chain registration document following ERC-8004 for agent metadata
* **Requires** an existing MPL Core asset and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` SDK

## Quick Start

1. Prerequisites — Get an MPL Core asset and install the SDK
2. Register an Agent — Call `registerIdentityV1` to bind identity
3. Agent Registration Document — Create the off-chain metadata JSON
4. Verify Registration — Confirm the identity was attached
5. Full Example — End-to-end code sample

## What You'll Learn

This guide shows you how to register an agent with:

* An identity record linked to an MPL Core asset
* A PDA (Program Derived Address) that makes the agent discoverable on-chain
* An AgentIdentity plugin with lifecycle hooks for Transfer, Update, and Execute

## Prerequisites

You need an MPL Core asset before registering. If you don't have one yet, see Create an NFT. For more on the identity program itself, see the MPL Agent Registry docs.

## Register an Agent

Registration creates a PDA derived from the asset's public key and attaches an `AgentIdentity` plugin with lifecycle hooks for Transfer, Update, and Execute. The PDA makes agents discoverable — anyone can derive it from an asset address and check whether it has a registered identity.

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { mplAgentIdentity } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { registerIdentityV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentIdentity());

await registerIdentityV1(umi, {

  asset: assetPublicKey,

  collection: collectionPublicKey,

  agentRegistrationUri: 'https://example.com/agent-registration.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

## Parameters

| Parameter            | Description                                           |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| asset                | The MPL Core asset to register                        |
| collection           | The asset's collection (optional)                     |
| agentRegistrationUri | URI pointing to off-chain agent registration metadata |
| payer                | Pays for rent and fees (defaults to umi.payer)        |
| authority            | Collection authority (defaults to payer)              |

## Agent Registration Document

The `agentRegistrationUri` points to a JSON document describing the agent's identity, services, and metadata. The format follows ERC-8004 adapted for Solana. Upload the JSON (and any associated image) to a permanent storage provider like Arweave so it's publicly accessible. For programmatic uploads, see this guide.

`{

  "type": "https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004#registration-v1",

  "name": "Plexpert",

  "description": "An informational agent providing help related to Metaplex protocols and tools.",

  "image": "https://arweave.net/agent-avatar-tx-hash",

  "services": [

    {

      "name": "web",

      "endpoint": "https://metaplex.com/agent/<ASSET_PUBKEY>"

    },

    {

      "name": "A2A",

      "endpoint": "https://metaplex.com/agent/<ASSET_PUBKEY>/agent-card.json",

      "version": "0.3.0"

    },

    {

      "name": "MCP",

      "endpoint": "https://metaplex.com/agent/<ASSET_PUBKEY>/mcp",

      "version": "2025-06-18"

    }

  ],

  "active": true,

  "registrations": [

    {

      "agentId": "<MINT_ADDRESS>",

      "agentRegistry": "solana:101:metaplex"

    }

  ],

  "supportedTrust": [

    "reputation",

    "crypto-economic"

  ]

}

`

### Fields

| Field          | Required | Description                                                                                         |
| -------------- | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type           | Yes      | Schema identifier. Use https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004#registration-v1.                     |
| name           | Yes      | Human-readable agent name                                                                           |
| description    | Yes      | Natural language description of the agent — what it does, how it works, and how to interact with it |
| image          | Yes      | Avatar or logo URI                                                                                  |
| services       | No       | Array of service endpoints the agent exposes (see below)                                            |
| active         | No       | Whether the agent is currently active (true/false)                                                  |
| registrations  | No       | Array of on-chain registrations linking back to this agent's identity                               |
| supportedTrust | No       | Trust models the agent supports (e.g. reputation, crypto-economic, tee-attestation)                 |

### Services

Each service entry describes a way to interact with the agent:

| Field    | Required | Description                                            |
| -------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| name     | Yes      | Service type — e.g. web, A2A, MCP, OASF, DID, email    |
| endpoint | Yes      | URL or identifier where the service can be reached     |
| version  | No       | Protocol version                                       |
| skills   | No       | Array of skills the agent exposes through this service |
| domains  | No       | Array of domains the agent operates in                 |

### Registrations

Each registration entry links back to an on-chain identity record:

| Field         | Required | Description                                            |
| ------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| agentId       | Yes      | The agent's mint address                               |
| agentRegistry | Yes      | Constant registry identifier — use solana:101:metaplex |

## Verify Registration

`import { fetchAsset } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

const assetData = await fetchAsset(umi, assetPublicKey);

// Check the AgentIdentity plugin

const agentIdentity = assetData.agentIdentities?.[0];

console.log(agentIdentity?.uri);               // your registration URI

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.transfer);  // truthy

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.update);    // truthy

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.execute);   // truthy

`

## Full Example

`import { generateSigner } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi';

import { create, createCollection } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

import { registerIdentityV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

// 1. Create a collection

const collection = generateSigner(umi);

await createCollection(umi, {

  collection,

  name: 'Agent Collection',

  uri: 'https://example.com/collection.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 2. Create an asset

const asset = generateSigner(umi);

await create(umi, {

  asset,

  name: 'My Agent',

  uri: 'https://example.com/agent.json',

  collection,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 3. Register identity

await registerIdentityV1(umi, {

  asset: asset.publicKey,

  collection: collection.publicKey,

  agentRegistrationUri: 'https://example.com/agent-registration.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

## Notes

* Registration is a one-time operation per asset. Calling `registerIdentityV1` on an already-registered asset will fail.
* The `agentRegistrationUri` should point to permanently hosted JSON (e.g. Arweave). If the URI becomes unreachable, the on-chain identity still exists but clients won't be able to fetch the agent's metadata.
* The `collection` parameter is optional but recommended — it enables collection-level authority checks during registration.
* Lifecycle hooks for Transfer, Update, and Execute are automatically attached. These hooks allow the identity plugin to participate in approving or rejecting operations on the asset.

# Read Agent Data

Last updated March 12, 2026

Ask AI

Skill file available

Read and verify agent identity data on-chain after registration. 

## Summary

Read agent identity data, verify registration status, inspect the AgentIdentity plugin, fetch the off-chain registration document, and derive the agent's built-in wallet address.

* **Check registration** using `safeFetchAgentIdentityV1` which returns `null` for unregistered assets
* **Inspect the AgentIdentity plugin** directly on the fetched Core asset for URI and lifecycle hooks
* **Fetch the registration document** from the on-chain URI to read agent metadata and service endpoints
* **Derive the agent's wallet** using `findAssetSignerPda` — a PDA with no private key that holds the agent's funds

## Check Registration

The safe fetch method returns `null` instead of throwing if the identity doesn't exist, which is useful for checking whether an asset has been registered:

`import { safeFetchAgentIdentityV1, findAgentIdentityV1Pda } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const pda = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: assetPublicKey });

const identity = await safeFetchAgentIdentityV1(umi, pda);

console.log('Registered:', identity !== null);

`

## Fetch from Seeds

You can also fetch the identity directly from the asset's public key without manually deriving the PDA:

`import { fetchAgentIdentityV1FromSeeds } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const identity = await fetchAgentIdentityV1FromSeeds(umi, {

  asset: assetPublicKey,

});

`

## Verify the AgentIdentity Plugin

Registration attaches an `AgentIdentity` plugin to the Core asset. You can read it directly off the fetched asset to inspect the registration URI and lifecycle hooks:

`import { fetchAsset } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

const assetData = await fetchAsset(umi, assetPublicKey);

const agentIdentity = assetData.agentIdentities?.[0];

console.log(agentIdentity?.uri);               // registration URI

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.transfer);  // truthy

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.update);    // truthy

console.log(agentIdentity?.lifecycleChecks?.execute);   // truthy

`

## Read the Registration Document

The `uri` on the `AgentIdentity` plugin points to an off-chain JSON document with the agent's full profile — name, description, service endpoints, and more. Fetch it like any other URI:

`import { fetchAsset } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

const assetData = await fetchAsset(umi, assetPublicKey);

const agentIdentity = assetData.agentIdentities?.[0];

if (agentIdentity?.uri) {

  const response = await fetch(agentIdentity.uri);

  const registration = await response.json();

  console.log(registration.name);          // "Plexpert"

  console.log(registration.description);   // "An informational agent..."

  console.log(registration.active);        // true

  for (const service of registration.services) {

    console.log(service.name);             // "web", "A2A", "MCP", etc.

    console.log(service.endpoint);         // service URL

    console.log(service.version);          // protocol version (if set)

  }

}

`

The document follows the ERC-8004 agent registration standard. A typical one looks like this:

`{

  "type": "https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004#registration-v1",

  "name": "An informational agent providing help related to Metaplex protocols and tools.",

  "description": "An autonomous agent that executes DeFi strategies on Solana.",

  "image": "https://arweave.net/agent-avatar-tx-hash",

  "services": [

    {

      "name": "web",

      "endpoint": "https://metaplex.com/agent/<ASSET_PUBKEY>"

    },

    {

      "name": "A2A",

      "endpoint": "https://metaplex.com/agent/<ASSET_PUBKEY>/agent-card.json",

      "version": "0.3.0"

    }

  ],

  "active": true,

  "registrations": [

    {

      "agentId": "<MINT_ADDRESS>",

      "agentRegistry": "solana:101:metaplex"

    }

  ],

  "supportedTrust": ["reputation", "crypto-economic"]

}

`

See Register an Agent for the full field reference.

## Fetch the Agent's Wallet

Every Core asset has a built-in wallet called the **Asset Signer** — a PDA derived from the asset's public key. No private key exists, so it can't be stolen. The wallet can hold SOL, tokens, or any other asset. Derive the address with `findAssetSignerPda`:

`import { findAssetSignerPda } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

const assetSignerPda = findAssetSignerPda(umi, { asset: assetPublicKey });

const balance = await umi.rpc.getBalance(assetSignerPda);

console.log('Agent wallet:', assetSignerPda);

console.log('Balance:', balance.basisPoints.toString(), 'lamports');

`

The address is deterministic, so anyone can derive it from the asset's public key to send funds or check balances. Only the asset itself can sign for this wallet, through Core's Execute instruction via a delegated executive.

See the MPL Agent Registry smart contract docs for account layouts, PDA derivation details, and error codes.

## Notes

* The Asset Signer is a PDA — no private key exists for it. It can receive funds from any source, but only the asset itself can sign outgoing transactions through Core's Execute instruction.
* `safeFetchAgentIdentityV1` returns `null` for unregistered assets rather than throwing, making it safe for existence checks without try/catch.
* `findAssetSignerPda` derives the wallet address deterministically. The same address is returned regardless of network, so you can use it on devnet and mainnet with the same asset key.

# Run an Agent

Last updated March 12, 2026

Ask AI

Skill file available

Set up an executive profile and delegate execution to run an agent on Solana. 

## Summary

Execution delegation allows an off-chain executive to sign transactions on behalf of an agent asset, bridging the gap between on-chain identity and off-chain operation.

* **Register an executive profile** — a one-time on-chain setup per wallet that creates a verifiable operator identity
* **Delegate execution** — the asset owner links their agent to a specific executive via an on-chain delegation record
* **Verify delegation** — derive the delegation record PDA and check whether the account exists
* **Requires** a registered agent and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+)

## Quick Start

1. Why Delegation Is Needed — Understand the problem delegation solves
2. Register an Executive Profile — One-time setup per wallet
3. Delegate Execution — Link agent to executive
4. Verify Delegation — Confirm the delegation record exists
5. Full Example — End-to-end code sample

## Why Delegation Is Needed

Every Core asset has a built-in wallet (the Asset Signer) — a PDA with no private key, which means it can't be stolen. Only the asset itself can sign for that wallet through Core's Execute lifecycle hook.

The problem is that Solana doesn't support background tasks or on-chain inference. An agent can't wake up and submit its own transactions. Something off-chain has to do it. But the agent owner also shouldn't have to sit at their computer approving every action.

Execution delegation bridges this gap. The owner delegates to an **executive** — a trusted off-chain operator that signs transactions on the agent's behalf using the Execute hook. The owner controls _who_ runs their agent without needing to be online for every transaction.

## What Is an Executive?

An executive is an on-chain profile representing a wallet authorized to operate agent assets. Think of it as a service account: you register a wallet as an executive once, and then individual agent owners can delegate execution to it.

This separates **identity** (who the agent is) from **execution** (who operates it). An executive profile is a PDA derived from the wallet's public key — one per wallet. Delegation is per-asset: the agent owner creates a delegation record linking their agent to a specific executive. A single executive can run many agents, and an owner can switch executives without touching the agent's identity.

Every executive profile lives on-chain, so the registry acts as a verifiable directory. Anyone can enumerate profiles, see which agents an executive operates, and inspect delegation history. This lays the groundwork for a reputation layer where executives are rated based on their on-chain track record.

## Prerequisites

You need a registered agent with an identity record and AgentIdentity plugin, and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+).

## Register an Executive Profile

Before an executive can run any agent, it needs a profile. This is a one-time setup per wallet:

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { registerExecutiveV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentTools());

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

The profile PDA is derived from seeds `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so each wallet can only have one.

## Delegate Execution

With the executive profile in place, the agent asset owner can delegate execution to it. This creates a delegation record on-chain linking the agent to the executive:

`import { delegateExecutionV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { findAgentIdentityV1Pda, findExecutiveProfileV1Pda } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: agentAssetPublicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates that:

* The executive profile exists
* The agent asset is a valid MPL Core asset
* The agent has a registered identity
* The signer is the asset owner

## Parameters

### RegisterExecutiveV1

| Parameter | Description                                                     |
| --------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| payer     | Pays for rent and fees (also used as the authority)             |
| authority | The wallet that owns this executive profile (defaults to payer) |

### DelegateExecutionV1

| Parameter        | Description                                    |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| agentAsset       | The registered agent's MPL Core asset          |
| agentIdentity    | The agent identity PDA for the asset           |
| executiveProfile | The executive profile PDA to delegate to       |
| payer            | Pays for rent and fees (defaults to umi.payer) |
| authority        | Must be the asset owner (defaults to payer)    |

## Verify Delegation

To check whether a delegation exists, derive the delegation record PDA and see if the account exists:

`import {

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

  findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, {

  authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey,

});

const delegateRecord = findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda(umi, {

  executiveProfile,

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

});

const account = await umi.rpc.getAccount(delegateRecord);

console.log('Delegated:', account.exists);

`

## Full Example

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { generateSigner } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi';

import { create, createCollection } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

import { mplAgentIdentity, mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import {

  registerIdentityV1,

  registerExecutiveV1,

  delegateExecutionV1,

  findAgentIdentityV1Pda,

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentIdentity())

  .use(mplAgentTools());

// 1. Create a collection

const collection = generateSigner(umi);

await createCollection(umi, {

  collection,

  name: 'Agent Collection',

  uri: 'https://example.com/collection.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 2. Create an asset

const asset = generateSigner(umi);

await create(umi, {

  asset,

  name: 'My Agent',

  uri: 'https://example.com/agent.json',

  collection,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 3. Register identity

await registerIdentityV1(umi, {

  asset: asset.publicKey,

  collection: collection.publicKey,

  agentRegistrationUri: 'https://example.com/agent-registration.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 4. Register executive profile

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 5. Delegate execution

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: asset.publicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: umi.payer.publicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: asset.publicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

## Notes

* Each wallet can only have one executive profile. The PDA is derived from `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so calling `registerExecutiveV1` again with the same wallet will fail.
* Delegation is per-asset — an owner must create a separate delegation record for each agent they want the executive to operate.
* Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates ownership on-chain.
* An owner can switch executives by creating a new delegation record with a different executive profile.

See the Agent Tools smart contract reference for account layouts, PDA derivation details, and error codes.

## What "Create agent" now is

**SDK — `@leash/registry-utils`:**
- `createAgent(umi, input, config?)` — one-shot wrapper around Metaplex's `mintAndSubmitAgent`. Mints a Core asset + registers Agent Identity in one tx. Returns `{ assetAddress, signature, network }`.
- `prepareAgentMint` / `sendPreparedAgentMint` — two-step variant for priority fees / hardware wallets.
- `getAgentIdentityStatus(umi, asset)` — `safeFetchAgentIdentityV1FromSeeds` + `findAssetSignerPda` in one call. Returns `registered: false` instead of throwing for unregistered assets, exactly matching the docs' guidance.
- `getAgentTreasury`, `loadAgentRegistration` — read helpers.
- `registerExecutive`, `hasExecutiveProfile`, `delegateExecution`, `isExecutionDelegated` — full delegation surface from `mpl-agent-tools`.

**Web playground:**
- `/agents/new` — full Create Agent form: name, description, on-chain `uri`, network, services, supported trust. Submits to `POST /api/agents/create`, persists the new mint to local storage, links to the new profile.
- `/agents` — top-right "Create agent" button + sidebar `Create agent` entry. Existing "Track" form still works for assets you already own.
- `/agents/[mint]` — added an **Identity** stat card (registered / not registered + on-chain owner), **treasury SOL balance** under the Asset Signer PDA card, and a new **Execute (delegation)** tab with `registerExecutiveV1` + `delegateExecutionV1` buttons that call live API routes and reflect on-chain state.
- API routes: `/api/agents/create`, `/api/agents/identity`, `/api/agents/balance`, `/api/agents/executive` (GET status + POST register/delegate).

**Signing model (transparent in the UI):** `apps/web/lib/umi.ts` reads `LEASH_DEV_PAYER_SECRET_KEY` (base58 or JSON byte array) and uses it as `keypairIdentity`. That wallet pays gas and becomes the asset owner. Documented in `apps/web/.env.local.example` and on the Create Agent page.

**Docs:** new `guides/create-an-agent.mdx` (wired into `docs.json`), expanded `sdk/registry-utils.mdx` with all new exports grouped by purpose, and `concepts/agent.mdx` now describes the Create / Read / Run lifecycle.

## Are we missing anything? Yes — and honestly

Per Metaplex's three docs (`mint-agent`, `register-agent`, `read-agent-data`, `run-an-agent`), here's the full picture:

| Capability | What it is | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Mint** new agent (Core asset + Identity in one tx) | `mintAndSubmitAgent` | ✅ `createAgent` |
| **Register** identity on existing asset (BYO) | `registerIdentityV1` | ✅ `registerAgentIdentity` (pre-existing) |
| **Read** identity / treasury / registration JSON | `safeFetchAgentIdentityV1FromSeeds`, `findAssetSignerPda`, fetch URI | ✅ `getAgentIdentityStatus`, `getAgentTreasury`, `loadAgentRegistration` |
| **Register Executive** profile (per wallet, one-time) | `registerExecutiveV1` | ✅ `registerExecutive` + UI button |
| **Delegate Execution** (per asset, owner-signed) | `delegateExecutionV1` | ✅ `delegateExecution` + UI button + delegation status check |
| **Revoke** delegation | `revokeExecutionV1` | ❌ missing — easy add, mirrors `delegateExecution` |
| **Compose `Execute`** instructions to actually move funds (SPL `transferChecked`, SOL transfer, arbitrary CPI signed by Asset Signer PDA) | `mpl-core` `Execute` ix from the executive | ❌ stubbed — `treasury/withdraw.ts` returns the descriptor only. **This is the actual gap that prevents an agent from doing anything useful.** |
| **Set agent token** (link an SPL mint to the agent for ownership-style access) | `setAgentTokenV1` | ❌ not exposed (Metaplex's "Create an Agent Token" guide) |
| **Verify** logic / reputation hooks (validation + reputation programs from the SDK) | `mpl-agent-validation`, `mpl-agent-reputation` | ❌ not exposed |
| **AppData / pause** read on-chain (so the kill switch survives a runner restart) | Core AppData plugin | ⚠️ env-var only today |

**The single missing thing that would make agents truly useful**: composing the `Execute` instruction so a delegated executive can spend the treasury (e.g. send USDC, swap, CPI a DeFi program). The plumbing under it is now in place — `delegateExecution` + the treasury PDA derivation are both shipped — but `@leash/core/treasury/withdraw.ts` still returns a descriptor instead of a `TransactionBuilder`. That's the next focused PR.

If you want, I can take a swing at the `Execute`-instruction composer next: a `withdrawSpl({ asset, mint, amount, destination, executive })` helper plus a "Spend from treasury" UI on the agent profile.

References used: [Mint an Agent](https://www.metaplex.com/docs/agents/mint-agent), [Register an Agent](https://www.metaplex.com/docs/agents/register-agent), [Read Agent Data](https://www.metaplex.com/docs/agents/read-agent-data), [Run an Agent](https://www.metaplex.com/docs/agents/run-an-agent).

## What you get now

**Server (`/api/agents/balance`)** — single endpoint, single round-trip per refresh, returns:

```json
{
  "asset": "...",
  "treasury": "<Asset Signer PDA>",
  "network": "devnet",
  "sol": 0.1234,
  "lamports": "123400000",
  "tokens": [
    { "symbol": "USDC", "name": "USD Coin (devnet)", "decimals": 6, "amount": "1000000", "ui": 1, "program": "spl-token", "known": true, "mint": "..." },
    { "symbol": "USDT", ... },
    { "symbol": "USDG", ..., "program": "spl-token-2022" },
    { "symbol": null, "name": null, ..., "known": false, "mint": "<unknown>" }
  ]
}
```

Internally it fans out three RPC calls in parallel: `getBalance` (SOL), and `getTokenAccountsByOwner` for both `TOKEN_PROGRAM_ID` *and* `TOKEN_2022_PROGRAM_ID` (USDG is Token-2022 on mainnet). Then merges with a `pinnedMints` list so USDC / USDT / USDG always appear — even at zero — so the UI is never empty when an agent is freshly funded.

**Known-token registry (`apps/web/lib/known-tokens.ts`)** — kept in the web app since it's UX, not protocol. Mainnet covers USDC, USDT, USDG (Token-2022), BONK, JUP, wSOL; devnet covers Circle's official USDC + a common community USDT + wSOL. Network is auto-detected from `SOLANA_RPC` (devnet/localhost regex).

**Profile UI** — the small "Treasury (Asset Signer PDA)" stat tile is replaced with a **Treasury value** summary (e.g. `0.1234 SOL + 2 tokens`) and a new full-width **Treasury balances** card below the stats:

- Each row: token icon (first 4 letters), symbol + name, mint subtitle, formatted UI amount with decimals.
- Token-2022 mints get an `Token-2022` outline badge so you can tell them apart visually.
- Unknown mints are kept in the list (greyed icon, mint shown as fallback) so you don't lose track of weird airdrops.
- Treasury PDA is shown once at the top of the card with a copy button.
- Refresh button + 8-second polling.
- Sort: known tokens first → highest UI balance → alphabetical.

**No new dependencies.** SOL still uses `umi.rpc.getBalance`; SPL queries hit Solana JSON-RPC directly via `fetch`, which keeps the web app's dep tree exactly as it was.

If you later want to add USDH, PYUSD, or any other token, the registry is just a JSON-style object in `lib/known-tokens.ts` — no protocol or API changes needed.

### What changed

**1. Two metadata modes on `/agents/new`** — a tab toggle right above the URI section:

- **Pin to Pinata for me** (default): the form builds `{ name, description, image? }` from your inputs, POSTs it to a new server route `/api/registry/pin`, which calls `pinJson` from `@leash/registry-utils` using `PINATA_JWT` (server-only). The Mint Succeeded card now shows the resolved URI (clickable, opens in a new tab) with a `pinned` / `byo` badge so you can verify what landed on-chain.

**2. Supported trust is now a checkbox group** — and answers your question explicitly. From the [MIP-104 / ERC-8004](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004) spec, `supportedTrust` is the field where an agent declares **which trust mechanisms back its claims**. Pick all that apply:

| Value             | Meaning                                                                          |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `reputation`      | Community feedback / on-chain reputation. Trust by track record.                 |
| `crypto-economic` | Bonded stake / slashing — misbehaviour costs the operator money.                 |
| `tee`             | Runs inside a Trusted Execution Environment with verifiable attestation.         |
| `zk-proof`        | Publishes zero-knowledge proofs of correct execution.                            |

Buyers and registries filter by these when picking agents, so an empty list signals "trust nothing about me yet" — fine for prototypes, weak for production. The UI shows a yellow warning when nothing is selected. There's also a custom-add input below the presets for cases that don't fit (`audit-firm-x`, `proof-of-humanity`, etc.).

**3. Env + docs updated.**
- `apps/web/.env.local.example` now documents the optional `PINATA_JWT` (and `PINATA_GATEWAY`) with a link to where to get it.
- `apps/docs/guides/create-an-agent.mdx` got a new "What 'Supported trust' means" section and an updated "In the playground" section explaining both metadata modes.

To use Pinata mode locally: grab a JWT from [app.pinata.cloud → API Keys](https://app.pinata.cloud), drop it in `apps/web/.env.local` as `PINATA_JWT=...`, restart `pnpm dev`. If unset, the API route returns a friendly 503 telling you what to do instead of failing silently.

## What changed

You were exactly right — buyer-kit got the `onReceipt` treatment last round, but seller-kit was left as a dumb 402 gate. That's now fixed and the explorer feed is symmetric.

### `@leash/seller-kit`
- **`createSeller`** now accepts `onReceipt` and `policyVersion`, mirroring `BuyerConfig`. After every settled call (status 2xx/3xx, post-gate), it builds an `earn` `ReceiptV1`, hash-chains it (`prev_receipt_hash` per agent), increments a per-agent nonce, and ships it to your callback. `onReceipt` errors are intentionally swallowed so a runner outage never breaks a paying customer.
- **402 → no receipt.** **5xx after the gate → no receipt.** No false trades.
- **`parsePrice()`** new helper — turns `"$0.001"`, `"0.5 USDC"`, or bare `"0.01"` into `{ amount, currency }` (normalising `$`/`USD` to USDC since SVM settlement is USDC).
- **11 tests** covering: gate 402/200, valid `earn` receipt schema, no-emit-on-402, hash chain across 3 calls, no-emit-on-500, callback-error-swallow, and price-parser edge cases.

### Demos & playground (now ship `earn` receipts to the runner)
- `apps/seller-demo` — wires `onReceipt` → `POST ${RUNNER_URL}/a/:agent/receipts`. Added `@leash/schemas` dep.
- `apps/merged-demo` — same wiring (it already had `postReceipt` for the buyer side; now both sides use it).
- `apps/web/api/seller/echo` — was a hand-rolled 402 mock. **Now uses real `seller-kit`** via `app.fetch(req)` so the playground produces honest `earn` receipts. Accepts `?asset=<mint>` to attribute.
- `apps/web/buyer/page.tsx` — when firing at the built-in seller, appends `?asset=${agent}` so both `spend` and `earn` land under the same agent and the explorer at `/agents/[mint]` fills from both sides of the trade.

### Drive-by fixes (CI was already broken on `main`)
- `packages/registry-utils/src/pin-file.ts` — TS strict-mode rejected the `Uint8Array.buffer` slice (could be `SharedArrayBuffer`). Now copies into a fresh `ArrayBuffer`.
- `apps/web/app/agents/new/page.tsx` — replaced a `// eslint-disable @next/next/no-img-element` for a rule that isn't loaded in the eslint config.

That hydration warning is purely from browser extensions (Grammarly and ColorZilla) injecting attributes onto `<body>` before React hydrates — `cz-shortcut-listen`, `data-new-gr-c-s-check-loaded`, `data-gr-ext-installed`. None of those are in your code; they're added by the extension at document-load time.

The standard fix is `suppressHydrationWarning` on the affected element. It only suppresses the diff at that single node (children still validate normally), so it's exactly the right tool here.

[REDACTED]

Done. Reload the `/seller` page — the warning will be gone.

A note on what this does and doesn't do, since the flag has a reputation for being misused:

- It silences attribute mismatches **only on `<html>` and `<body>`** — exactly the two nodes extensions touch. Any real hydration bug inside your app (a `Date.now()` in a component, a stale SSR snapshot, etc.) will still be flagged loud and clear.
- It does **not** disable hydration; React still hydrates normally.
- It's the official Next.js / React recommendation for this exact extension-injection problem (see the [React docs](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/client/hydrateRoot#suppressing-unavoidable-hydration-mismatch-errors)).

The three culprits in your stack:
- `cz-shortcut-listen="true"` — ColorZilla
- `data-new-gr-c-s-check-loaded` / `data-gr-ext-installed` — Grammarly

Nothing you wrote was wrong; the page is fine.

Applying an optimistic SWR cache update after successful `registerExecutive` / `delegateExecution`, then revalidating so the badges update immediately even if RPC lags briefly.

[REDACTED]

Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
After a successful tx you already called `await refetchExec()`, which re-fetches from `/api/agents/executive`. The RPC helpers (`hasExecutiveProfile` / `isExecutionDelegated`) can still return the **old** state for a short time right after confirmation, so the UI could stay on “not registered” / “not delegated” until a later refresh.

### Fix
Right after a successful `registerExecutive` or `delegateExecution`, we now **`mutate` the executive SWR cache with the known-good flags** and still **`revalidate: true`** so the server catches up when the chain is ready:

- **Register** → set `registered: true`, keep existing `delegated`.
- **Delegate** → set `delegated: true`, keep `registered` (default to `true` if there was no prior snapshot).

So the badges flip **immediately** on success, then the background revalidation replaces cache with on-chain truth when RPC is consistent.

Change is in `callExec` in ```103:145:leash/apps/web/app/agents/[mint]/page.tsx``` (approximate line range after edit).

Found both issues. Let me explain then fix.

### Issue 1 — `/api/registry/resolve` returns 404

The Metaplex API at that URL returns **HTTP 200** with a perfectly good JSON document — but with `type: "agent"`. Our `RegistrationV1Schema` requires `type: "https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004#registration-v1"`. `resolveByoUri` throws `InvalidSchemaError` on the mismatch and our route blanket-maps **any** thrown error to **404**, so a 200-but-not-MIP-104 looks like “not found”.

It’s not a real 404 and the auto-populated URI from the on-chain plugin **is** the Metaplex agent-metadata URL (not a separate ERC-8004 doc). Fix: return the raw JSON whether or not it validates, with a `valid` flag and a clearer status code.

### Issue 2 — Register/Delegate badges don’t flip immediately

The optimistic mutate I added passes `revalidate: true`, which immediately re-fetches `/api/agents/executive`. RPC sees the previous slot and answers “not registered / not delegated”, **overwriting** the optimistic value. A page refresh later works because by then the read endpoint sees the new state.

Fix: skip the immediate revalidation and schedule one a few seconds out so RPC has caught up.

Both fixed. Quick recap of what was actually wrong vs. what you saw:

### `/api/registry/resolve` 404
- **Looked like:** the URL was bad / agent missing.
- **Actually:** the upstream Metaplex API returned **HTTP 200** with a fine JSON document, but it has `type: "agent"`. Our `RegistrationV1Schema` requires `type: "https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8004#registration-v1"`, so `safeParse` failed. The route then mapped any thrown error to **404**, hiding the real reason.
- **Now:** route fetches and returns the raw `document` always, plus `valid: boolean` and `schemaError`. Status codes are honest: `400` bad query, `502` upstream-broken, `200` otherwise. The Identity tab shows a green “RegistrationV1 (MIP-104) valid” badge when strict, and an amber “non-strict shape” badge for Metaplex’s shape — with the raw JSON either way.

### Register / Delegate showing stale state
- **Looked like:** the call did nothing.
- **Actually:** I had set `revalidate: true` on the optimistic mutate, which immediately re-fetched `/api/agents/executive`. Solana RPC was still a slot or two behind the just-confirmed tx, returned `registered/delegated: false`, and that **overwrote** the optimistic value in cache. A page refresh later worked because by then RPC had caught up.
- **Now:** optimistic mutate uses `revalidate: false`, then a `setTimeout(refetchExec, 4000)` schedules the real revalidation once chain state is consistent. Badge flips to `registered` / `delegated` instantly and stays there; the delayed refresh reconciles silently.

If 4 s ever feels too slow on your RPC, drop it to 2 s — it’s a single number in `callExec`.

Here’s how the two playgrounds fit together in plain terms.

## Buyer playground (`/buyer`)

**What it is**  
A UI around `@leash/buyer-kit`’s `createBuyer`. You define a **`RulesV1`** policy (budget, allowed hosts, trigger interval, etc.) and a **target URL**. When you click “Fire request”, the browser does **not** run the buyer in the page; it **`POST`s to `/api/buyer/fire`** with `agent`, `rules`, `url`, `method`, and optional `body`.

**What the server does**  
The API route builds `createBuyer({ agent, rules, onReceipt })` and calls **`.fetch(url, init)`**. Inside the kit:

1. **Policy** — `evaluate` checks the request (host, budget, etc.). If it **denies**, you still get a **`spend`** receipt with `decision: 'deny'` and no HTTP call to the seller.
2. **If allowed** — it runs something like **`x402Fetch`**: first request may get **402 Payment Required**; the mock layer adds **`x-payment: mock`** and retries so the seller can answer **200**.
3. **Receipt** — a finalized **`ReceiptV1`** with `kind: 'spend'` is produced for the **buyer agent** (`agent` field = the mint you typed in).
4. **Runner** — `onReceipt` **`POST`s that receipt** to `LEASH_RUNNER_URL/a/<agent>/receipts` so the agent’s receipt feed updates.

**Default URL trick**  
If the URL is the built-in **`/api/seller/echo`** and you didn’t add `?asset=…`, the page **appends `?asset=<your Agent mint>`** so the **seller’s `earn` receipt** is attributed to the **same mint** you’re testing on `/agents/[mint]` (both sides of the “trade” show up there).

**What you see**  
Latest HTTP status + response body + the **`spend`** receipt JSON, plus session history. A link goes to **`/agents/<mint>`** to inspect receipts on the profile page.

---

## Seller playground (`/seller`)

**What it is**  
A direct way to hit the **same** built-in seller route the buyer uses: **`POST /api/seller/echo`**. That route is implemented with **`createSeller`** from `@leash/seller-kit` (Hono + **`simpleX402Gate`**: no **`x-payment`** → **402**; with header → handler runs).

**What you configure**  
- **Seller agent (mint)** — which Core asset is “selling”; this becomes **`?asset=<mint>`** on the request so **`earn`** receipts use that mint. Chips load from **`leash:web:agents`** in localStorage (agents you created in the app).  
- **Include `x-payment` or not** — demos the gate: **402** vs **200**.  
- **JSON body** — echoed back in the success response.

**What happens on success (200 + payment)**  
The seller kit emits an **`earn`** **`ReceiptV1`** for that **`asset`** and the **`onReceipt`** hook **`POST`s it** to the runner at **`/a/<asset>/receipts`**, same as the buyer path.

**What you see**  
HTTP status, response JSON, badges (“earn receipt emitted” when applicable), link to **`/agents/<mint>`**, and a short session history.

---

## How they relate

| | **Buyer** | **Seller** |
|---|-----------|------------|
| **Role** | Policy + paid HTTP client | Paid HTTP endpoint |
| **Receipt** | `kind: 'spend'` for the **buyer** mint | `kind: 'earn'` for the **seller** mint |
| **Payment** | Mock `x-payment` after 402 | Requires `x-payment` to proceed |
| **Typical demo** | Buyer calls **`/api/seller/echo`** | Same route, hit from the seller page |

So: **buyer** = “agent spends under rules and proves it with a receipt”; **seller** = “endpoint charges a mock x402 toll and proves settlement with an earn receipt”; both can **feed the same runner + `/agents/[mint]` explorer** when you align the mints (default buyer URL behavior + seller `asset` field).

## README.md

Outline

# X402 TypeScript Examples

This directory contains a collection of TypeScript examples demonstrating how to use the X402 protocol in various contexts. These examples are designed to work with the X402 npm packages and share a workspace with the main X402 packages.

## Setup

Before running any examples, you need to install dependencies and build the packages:

# From the examples/typescript directory
pnpm install
pnpm build

## Example Structure

The examples are organized into several categories:

### Clients

Examples of different client implementations for interacting with X402 services:

* `clients/axios/` \- Axios client with x402 payment interceptor from `x402-axios`.
* `clients/fetch/` \- Client using the `x402-fetch` wrapper around the native fetch API.
* `clients/cdp-sdk/` \- Client that uses CDP Server Wallets as the signer with `x402-axios`.
* `clients/chainlink-vrf-nft/` \- Example using Chainlink to mint a randomized NFT (see them on Opensea). Demonstrates verify/settle flow with `x402-axios`.

### Agents

* `agent/` \- Anthropic agent that pays via a Go proxy using `x402-fetch`.
* `dynamic_agent/` \- Agent that discovers tools dynamically and pays per-request using x402.

### Discovery

* `discovery/` \- Uses the facilitator to list available x402-protected resources (Bazaar).

### MCP

* `mcp/` \- MCP server that makes paid API requests via `x402-axios` (Claude Desktop compatible).
* `mcp-embedded-wallet/` \- Electron-based MCP server with an embedded wallet that signs requests via IPC.

### Facilitator

* `facilitator/` \- Example implementation of an x402 payment facilitator exposing `/verify` and `/settle`.

### Fullstack

* `fullstack/next/` \- Next.js app demonstrating route protection with `x402-next` middleware.
* `fullstack/mainnet/` \- Next.js app configured for Base mainnet using the Coinbase hosted facilitator.
* `fullstack/next-advanced/` \- \[WIP\] Deep Next.js integration using a paywall + session cookie after verify/settle.
* `fullstack/browser-wallet-example/` \- Browser wallet template: Hono server + React client with session and one-time payments.
* `fullstack/farcaster-miniapp/` \- Farcaster Mini App template with x402-protected APIs using MiniKit.
* `fullstack/auth_based_pricing/` \- SIWE + JWT with conditional pricing ($0.01 with JWT vs $0.10 without) using x402.

### Servers

Examples of different server implementations:

* `servers/express/` \- Express.js server using `x402-express` middleware.
* `servers/hono/` \- Hono server using `x402-hono` middleware.
* `servers/advanced/` \- Express server without middleware: delayed settlement, dynamic pricing, multiple requirements.
* `servers/mainnet/` \- Server example for accepting real USDC on Base mainnet using the Coinbase hosted facilitator.

## Running Examples

Each example directory contains its own README with specific instructions for running that example. Navigate to the desired example directory and follow its instructions.

## Development

This workspace uses:

* pnpm for package management
* Turborepo for monorepo management
* TypeScript for type safety

The examples are designed to work with the main X402 packages, so they must be built before running any examples.

## A note on private keys

The examples in this folder commonly use private keys to sign messages. **Never put a private key with mainnet funds in a `.env` file**. This can result in keys getting checked into codebases and being drained.

There are many ways to generate a keypair to use exclusively for development, one way is via foundry:

```
# install foundry
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash

# generate a new wallet
cast w new

```

You can fund your new wallet on most networks via the testnet CDP Faucet, simply provide the address generated by cast.

I need to dig into the codebase to understand how Leash currently handles x402 integration and whether the mock implementation actually captures the real value proposition of these buyer/seller agents, then I can give them a straightforward assessment with actionable next steps.

So the x402 protocol works in two steps: the server responds with a 402 Payment Required status and includes payment requirements in JSON that specify the scheme, network, asset, and amount needed, then the client signs a payment authorization to fulfill those requirements.

On the Solana side, there's emerging support through packages like `@x402/svm` that handle SPL token transfers, though it's still less mature than the EVM implementation. The honest picture is that we've built out the receipt and identity layer cleanly with Privy signing and the Asset Signer PDA, and the HTTP exchange follows the x402 shape, but the actual wire format and on-chain settlement are still mocked out—the buyer just sends a mock header without signing anything, and the seller never verifies or calls a facilitator to settle.

To make it real, we'd need the server to emit proper `paymentRequirements`, the buyer to sign an actual SVM transaction through Privy, a facilitator call to verify and settle on devnet, and the receipt's `tx_sig` to be the real Solana signature. On the product side, the current demo shows a closed-loop local seller, but the broader value prop—that buyers can hit any x402 endpoint in the wild and sellers can create their own paid endpoints—isn't really demonstrated yet.

The concrete path forward has three parts: first, get the wire format right with proper 402 headers and payment requirements in the body; second, wire up real signing via Privy and a facilitator (PayAI for SVM or self-hosted); third, fix the UX so the buyer playground actually lets you target arbitrary URLs and the seller side lets you define and mint new paid endpoints instead of just hitting a fixed echo route. I should also point them to the specific packages from the x402 repo—the core library, x402-fetch for the buyer side, x402-hono for the seller (since we're already on Hono), and PayAI as the SVM facilitator.

The buyer URL field is actually already free-form, so technically you could point anywhere today—I just need to verify that's working as intended.

Honest answer first, then a concrete plan.

## Where we actually are vs. where the docs make it sound

**What is real today**
- The HTTP **shape** of x402: server returns `402` without `x-payment`, forwards when present. Receipts, identity, runner, Solscan links — all real.
- `payTo` derivation is real: the seller’s **Asset Signer PDA** is correctly returned via `findAssetSignerPda`. So if money were ever to settle, it would settle to the right address.
- Receipt chain (`spend` ↔ `earn`, hash chain, runner ingest, explorer feed) is real.

**What is still mock**
- **Buyer never signs anything.** It just sets `x-payment: 'mock'`. See [`packages/buyer-kit/src/create-buyer.ts:67`](leash/packages/buyer-kit/src/create-buyer.ts).
- **Seller never verifies or settles.** `simpleX402Gate` only checks header presence ([`packages/seller-kit/src/hono/simple-x402.ts`](leash/packages/seller-kit/src/hono/simple-x402.ts)).
- **No `paymentRequirements` in the 402 body.** A real x402 response contains a JSON describing scheme/network/asset/`payTo`/amount; we return `{ error: 'payment_required', protocol: 'x402-shaped' }`. A real x402 client would have nothing to sign against.
- **No facilitator.** No `/verify`, no `/settle`, no on-chain SPL transfer, no `X-PAYMENT-RESPONSE` header. `tx_sig` in receipts is `null` (or a mock string from the test facilitator).
- **`parse402Body` is literally a placeholder** ([`packages/core/src/x402/parse.ts`](leash/packages/core/src/x402/parse.ts)).

So it’s “x402-shaped” in HTTP, but it is **not** an x402 trade — there is no money moving and a real x402 buyer (e.g. `x402-fetch`) would reject our 402.

## Does the UI tell the actual product story?

Half-true. Two specific gaps:

**Buyer playground** is supposed to convey *“give your agent a wallet + rules, let it pay anything on the open internet.”* Today it ships pointed at `http://localhost:3000/api/seller/echo`. The URL field is free-form, so technically you can paste a real x402 URL — but the demo and copy don’t encourage that, and there’s no example list, no “try a real x402 endpoint” button, and the result panel doesn’t distinguish a **real settlement signature** from `null`/mock.

**Seller playground** is supposed to convey *“mint a payment link for your product, your agent collects.”* Today it just **hits** an existing fixed echo route with one hard-coded price (`$0.001`). There is no flow for a user to **define a paid endpoint** (price, route, schema, where receipts go). It demonstrates the *consuming* side of a seller, not the *creating* side. That’s the bigger product gap.

## Concrete plan to swap mock for real x402

The x402 reference packages you linked are EVM-first but have an SVM track. Here’s what I’d do, in dependency order, keying off [`x402-foundation/x402`](https://github.com/x402-foundation/x402/tree/main/examples/typescript):

### Layer 1 — Wire format (no chain yet, hours of work)
Make our 402 and our buyer protocol-correct so a real `x402-fetch` and a real `x402-hono` could already talk to us:
- **Seller:** return a proper [`paymentRequirements[]`](https://github.com/x402-foundation/x402) JSON in the 402 body — `scheme: 'exact'`, `network: 'solana-devnet'`, `asset: USDC mint`, `payTo: <Asset Signer PDA>`, `maxAmountRequired`, `resource`, `description`, `mimeType`.
- **Buyer:** drop our handcrafted `x402Fetch`. Use **[`x402-fetch`](https://github.com/x402-foundation/x402/tree/main/examples/typescript/clients/fetch)** and pass it a Solana signer. It will read `paymentRequirements`, sign, base64-encode into `X-PAYMENT`, retry, and read `X-PAYMENT-RESPONSE`.
- **Receipts:** populate `tx_sig` from `X-PAYMENT-RESPONSE.transaction`, populate `facilitator` from the URL we configured.

### Layer 2 — Real seller middleware (replace `simpleX402Gate`)
Adopt **[`x402-hono`](https://github.com/x402-foundation/x402/tree/main/examples/typescript/servers/hono)**’s `paymentMiddleware`. Our `createSeller` becomes a thin convenience around it that:
- still resolves `payTo` from the agent mint via `findAssetSignerPda`,
- still emits `earn` receipts from a `settled` event (the middleware exposes a hook).

This is the biggest deletion of code in the repo — it replaces our gate, parser placeholder, and `x402Fetch` with the upstream packages.

### Layer 3 — Real facilitator (real money moves on devnet)
Two paths, pick one for v0.1:
- **Hosted facilitator:** PayAI (we already cite it in seller-kit/README) or Corbits. Zero infra, but you’re trusting a third party in the demo.
- **Self-hosted SVM facilitator:** small Hono service implementing `/verify` and `/settle` for SPL `transferChecked` to `payTo` on devnet. ## The short version

- **Hosted facilitator:** **`facilitator.svmacc.tech`** ([SVM x402 docs](https://docs.svmacc.tech/)). Free, gasless from the caller's POV — the facilitator is the `feePayer` per the [exact-SVM spec](https://github.com/coinbase/x402/blob/main/specs/schemes/exact/scheme_exact_svm.md), so the buyer only spends USDC.

A couple of things to untangle, then a concrete plan and one decision I need from you before I rip through the codebase.

## Why you saw a 402 (not actually an error)

`POST /api/seller/echo … 402 in 6568ms` is the **correct** x402 behavior. The seller middleware is doing its job: "here's my offer, pay me first." No payment header was attached (you probed it directly from the seller playground), so no settlement happened, so no receipt got created — that's why `/agents/33Qv…/receipts` is empty. Receipts only land when a buyer actually settles.

What's wrong is the **framing**. Right now the playgrounds read like "internal Leash testbeds". You're describing them as **products**:

- **Seller playground = Payment-Link Builder.** Anyone signs in, picks one of their agents as the receiver, defines a route / price / description / response, and gets a shareable URL (`https://yourapp/x/<id>`) any other agent can buy from. Like Stripe Payment Links for x402.
- **Buyer playground = Autonomous-Agent Cockpit.** The agent itself holds keys and signs. The Privy-connected user is just the *human owner*. The owner picks one of their agents, the agent fires at any x402 URL (your own or someone else's), pays itself, and the receipt feed at `/agents/<mint>` fills up automatically.
- **Rules live with the agent**, set once at creation (or "limitless" if you skip them), and the buyer playground just *uses* them — no per-call rules form.

Getting Started

# Run an Agent

Last updated March 12, 2026

Ask AI

Skill file available

Set up an executive profile and delegate execution to run an agent on Solana. 

## Summary

Execution delegation allows an off-chain executive to sign transactions on behalf of an agent asset, bridging the gap between on-chain identity and off-chain operation.

* **Register an executive profile** — a one-time on-chain setup per wallet that creates a verifiable operator identity
* **Delegate execution** — the asset owner links their agent to a specific executive via an on-chain delegation record
* **Verify delegation** — derive the delegation record PDA and check whether the account exists
* **Requires** a registered agent and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+)

## Quick Start

1. Why Delegation Is Needed — Understand the problem delegation solves
2. Register an Executive Profile — One-time setup per wallet
3. Delegate Execution — Link agent to executive
4. Verify Delegation — Confirm the delegation record exists
5. Full Example — End-to-end code sample

## Why Delegation Is Needed

Every Core asset has a built-in wallet (the Asset Signer) — a PDA with no private key, which means it can't be stolen. Only the asset itself can sign for that wallet through Core's Execute lifecycle hook.

The problem is that Solana doesn't support background tasks or on-chain inference. An agent can't wake up and submit its own transactions. Something off-chain has to do it. But the agent owner also shouldn't have to sit at their computer approving every action.

Execution delegation bridges this gap. The owner delegates to an **executive** — a trusted off-chain operator that signs transactions on the agent's behalf using the Execute hook. The owner controls _who_ runs their agent without needing to be online for every transaction.

## What Is an Executive?

An executive is an on-chain profile representing a wallet authorized to operate agent assets. Think of it as a service account: you register a wallet as an executive once, and then individual agent owners can delegate execution to it.

This separates **identity** (who the agent is) from **execution** (who operates it). An executive profile is a PDA derived from the wallet's public key — one per wallet. Delegation is per-asset: the agent owner creates a delegation record linking their agent to a specific executive. A single executive can run many agents, and an owner can switch executives without touching the agent's identity.

Every executive profile lives on-chain, so the registry acts as a verifiable directory. Anyone can enumerate profiles, see which agents an executive operates, and inspect delegation history. This lays the groundwork for a reputation layer where executives are rated based on their on-chain track record.

## Prerequisites

You need a registered agent with an identity record and AgentIdentity plugin, and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+).

## Register an Executive Profile

Before an executive can run any agent, it needs a profile. This is a one-time setup per wallet:

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { registerExecutiveV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentTools());

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

The profile PDA is derived from seeds `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so each wallet can only have one.

## Delegate Execution

With the executive profile in place, the agent asset owner can delegate execution to it. This creates a delegation record on-chain linking the agent to the executive:

`import { delegateExecutionV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { findAgentIdentityV1Pda, findExecutiveProfileV1Pda } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: agentAssetPublicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates that:

* The executive profile exists
* The agent asset is a valid MPL Core asset
* The agent has a registered identity
* The signer is the asset owner

## Parameters

### RegisterExecutiveV1

| Parameter | Description                                                     |
| --------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| payer     | Pays for rent and fees (also used as the authority)             |
| authority | The wallet that owns this executive profile (defaults to payer) |

### DelegateExecutionV1

| Parameter        | Description                                    |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| agentAsset       | The registered agent's MPL Core asset          |
| agentIdentity    | The agent identity PDA for the asset           |
| executiveProfile | The executive profile PDA to delegate to       |
| payer            | Pays for rent and fees (defaults to umi.payer) |
| authority        | Must be the asset owner (defaults to payer)    |

## Verify Delegation

To check whether a delegation exists, derive the delegation record PDA and see if the account exists:

`import {

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

  findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, {

  authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey,

});

const delegateRecord = findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda(umi, {

  executiveProfile,

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

});

const account = await umi.rpc.getAccount(delegateRecord);

console.log('Delegated:', account.exists);

`

## Full Example

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { generateSigner } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi';

import { create, createCollection } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

import { mplAgentIdentity, mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import {

  registerIdentityV1,

  registerExecutiveV1,

  delegateExecutionV1,

  findAgentIdentityV1Pda,

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentIdentity())

  .use(mplAgentTools());

// 1. Create a collection

const collection = generateSigner(umi);

await createCollection(umi, {

  collection,

  name: 'Agent Collection',

  uri: 'https://example.com/collection.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 2. Create an asset

const asset = generateSigner(umi);

await create(umi, {

  asset,

  name: 'My Agent',

  uri: 'https://example.com/agent.json',

  collection,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 3. Register identity

await registerIdentityV1(umi, {

  asset: asset.publicKey,

  collection: collection.publicKey,

  agentRegistrationUri: 'https://example.com/agent-registration.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 4. Register executive profile

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 5. Delegate execution

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: asset.publicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: umi.payer.publicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: asset.publicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

## Notes

* Each wallet can only have one executive profile. The PDA is derived from `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so calling `registerExecutiveV1` again with the same wallet will fail.
* Delegation is per-asset — an owner must create a separate delegation record for each agent they want the executive to operate.
* Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates ownership on-chain.
* An owner can switch executives by creating a new delegation record with a different executive profile.

See the Agent Tools smart contract reference for account layouts, PDA derivation details, and error codes.

# Run an Agent

Last updated March 12, 2026

Ask AI

Skill file available

Set up an executive profile and delegate execution to run an agent on Solana. 

## Summary

Execution delegation allows an off-chain executive to sign transactions on behalf of an agent asset, bridging the gap between on-chain identity and off-chain operation.

* **Register an executive profile** — a one-time on-chain setup per wallet that creates a verifiable operator identity
* **Delegate execution** — the asset owner links their agent to a specific executive via an on-chain delegation record
* **Verify delegation** — derive the delegation record PDA and check whether the account exists
* **Requires** a registered agent and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+)

## Quick Start

1. Why Delegation Is Needed — Understand the problem delegation solves
2. Register an Executive Profile — One-time setup per wallet
3. Delegate Execution — Link agent to executive
4. Verify Delegation — Confirm the delegation record exists
5. Full Example — End-to-end code sample

## Why Delegation Is Needed

Every Core asset has a built-in wallet (the Asset Signer) — a PDA with no private key, which means it can't be stolen. Only the asset itself can sign for that wallet through Core's Execute lifecycle hook.

The problem is that Solana doesn't support background tasks or on-chain inference. An agent can't wake up and submit its own transactions. Something off-chain has to do it. But the agent owner also shouldn't have to sit at their computer approving every action.

Execution delegation bridges this gap. The owner delegates to an **executive** — a trusted off-chain operator that signs transactions on the agent's behalf using the Execute hook. The owner controls _who_ runs their agent without needing to be online for every transaction.

## What Is an Executive?

An executive is an on-chain profile representing a wallet authorized to operate agent assets. Think of it as a service account: you register a wallet as an executive once, and then individual agent owners can delegate execution to it.

This separates **identity** (who the agent is) from **execution** (who operates it). An executive profile is a PDA derived from the wallet's public key — one per wallet. Delegation is per-asset: the agent owner creates a delegation record linking their agent to a specific executive. A single executive can run many agents, and an owner can switch executives without touching the agent's identity.

Every executive profile lives on-chain, so the registry acts as a verifiable directory. Anyone can enumerate profiles, see which agents an executive operates, and inspect delegation history. This lays the groundwork for a reputation layer where executives are rated based on their on-chain track record.

## Prerequisites

You need a registered agent with an identity record and AgentIdentity plugin, and the `@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry` package (v0.2.0+).

## Register an Executive Profile

Before an executive can run any agent, it needs a profile. This is a one-time setup per wallet:

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { registerExecutiveV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentTools());

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

The profile PDA is derived from seeds `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so each wallet can only have one.

## Delegate Execution

With the executive profile in place, the agent asset owner can delegate execution to it. This creates a delegation record on-chain linking the agent to the executive:

`import { delegateExecutionV1 } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import { findAgentIdentityV1Pda, findExecutiveProfileV1Pda } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: agentAssetPublicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates that:

* The executive profile exists
* The agent asset is a valid MPL Core asset
* The agent has a registered identity
* The signer is the asset owner

## Parameters

### RegisterExecutiveV1

| Parameter | Description                                                     |
| --------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| payer     | Pays for rent and fees (also used as the authority)             |
| authority | The wallet that owns this executive profile (defaults to payer) |

### DelegateExecutionV1

| Parameter        | Description                                    |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| agentAsset       | The registered agent's MPL Core asset          |
| agentIdentity    | The agent identity PDA for the asset           |
| executiveProfile | The executive profile PDA to delegate to       |
| payer            | Pays for rent and fees (defaults to umi.payer) |
| authority        | Must be the asset owner (defaults to payer)    |

## Verify Delegation

To check whether a delegation exists, derive the delegation record PDA and see if the account exists:

`import {

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

  findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, {

  authority: executiveAuthorityPublicKey,

});

const delegateRecord = findExecutionDelegateRecordV1Pda(umi, {

  executiveProfile,

  agentAsset: agentAssetPublicKey,

});

const account = await umi.rpc.getAccount(delegateRecord);

console.log('Delegated:', account.exists);

`

## Full Example

`import { createUmi } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi-bundle-defaults';

import { generateSigner } from '@metaplex-foundation/umi';

import { create, createCollection } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-core';

import { mplAgentIdentity, mplAgentTools } from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

import {

  registerIdentityV1,

  registerExecutiveV1,

  delegateExecutionV1,

  findAgentIdentityV1Pda,

  findExecutiveProfileV1Pda,

} from '@metaplex-foundation/mpl-agent-registry';

const umi = createUmi('https://api.mainnet-beta.solana.com')

  .use(mplAgentIdentity())

  .use(mplAgentTools());

// 1. Create a collection

const collection = generateSigner(umi);

await createCollection(umi, {

  collection,

  name: 'Agent Collection',

  uri: 'https://example.com/collection.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 2. Create an asset

const asset = generateSigner(umi);

await create(umi, {

  asset,

  name: 'My Agent',

  uri: 'https://example.com/agent.json',

  collection,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 3. Register identity

await registerIdentityV1(umi, {

  asset: asset.publicKey,

  collection: collection.publicKey,

  agentRegistrationUri: 'https://example.com/agent-registration.json',

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 4. Register executive profile

await registerExecutiveV1(umi, {

  payer: umi.payer,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

// 5. Delegate execution

const agentIdentity = findAgentIdentityV1Pda(umi, { asset: asset.publicKey });

const executiveProfile = findExecutiveProfileV1Pda(umi, { authority: umi.payer.publicKey });

await delegateExecutionV1(umi, {

  agentAsset: asset.publicKey,

  agentIdentity,

  executiveProfile,

}).sendAndConfirm(umi);

`

## Notes

* Each wallet can only have one executive profile. The PDA is derived from `["executive_profile", <authority>]`, so calling `registerExecutiveV1` again with the same wallet will fail.
* Delegation is per-asset — an owner must create a separate delegation record for each agent they want the executive to operate.
* Only the asset owner can delegate execution. The program validates ownership on-chain.
* An owner can switch executives by creating a new delegation record with a different executive profile.

See the Agent Tools smart contract reference for account layouts, PDA derivation details, and error codes.

### About the 404
That `/api/endpoints?owner_agent=… → 404` was just the runner being down on `:8788` (your `apps/web/.env` points there). No process was listening — I started a fresh one in the background. After a hard refresh of the seller page the link list will load.

If you want it to start with the dev server every time, add it to your `pnpm dev` orchestration; for now I left it running on `:8788` writing to `./.leash/endpoints.jsonl`.

### What I added

**Schema (`EndpointV1`)** — three optional fields, all URL-validated:

```47:75:leash/packages/schemas/src/endpoint.ts
  /**
   * Optional post-payment redirect. When set, the seller responds with
   * `303 See Other` and a `Location` header pointing here, with
   * `?leash_tx=<sig>&leash_receipt=<hash>&leash_agent=<mint>` appended
   */
  redirect_url: HttpUrlSchema.optional(),
  webhook_url: HttpUrlSchema.optional(),
  wrap_receipt: z.boolean().default(false),
```

**`/x/[id]` route** — uses `AsyncLocalStorage` to capture the receipt from `seller-kit`'s `onReceipt` and apply post-processing inside the same response cycle:

- **Always**: stamps `X-Leash-Tx-Sig`, `X-Leash-Receipt-Hash`, `X-Leash-Agent`, `X-Leash-Tx-Explorer`, `X-Leash-Agent-Explorer` headers, plus `Access-Control-Expose-Headers` so browser clients can read them.
- **`redirect_url`**: returns `303 See Other` with `?leash_tx=&leash_receipt=&leash_agent=` appended so the destination page can verify server-side.
- **`wrap_receipt`**: wraps JSON responses as `{ data: <body>, _leash: { tx_sig, receipt_hash, agent, network, amount, facilitator, explorer: { tx, agent } } }`.
- **`webhook_url`**: fire-and-forget POST of `{ payment, response }`. Buyers can also send `x-leash-callback: <url>` per-call to fire one of their own (both fire if both set — useful for chaining one agent's output into a second agent).

**Seller UI** (`/seller`) — three new fields under "Post-payment hooks", plus `embeds receipt`/`↳ redirect`/`⇉ webhook` badges on each link in the list.

**Buyer UI** (`/buyer`) — new "Forward response to" field that sends `x-leash-callback`, plus a "Leash response headers" panel and a "Follow seller redirect →" button when the seller returns 303 (request fires with `redirect: 'manual'` so the buyer sees the 303 instead of auto-following).

**Tests + docs** — 2 new runner tests (round-trip the optional fields; reject `javascript:` URLs), regenerated `endpoint-v1.mdx` JSON Schema, expanded [`Create a payment link`](/Users/donatusprince/Desktop/projects/experimentals/leash/apps/docs/guides/create-an-endpoint.mdx) with full payload references, and a hooks summary in `runner.mdx`.

All 8 endpoint tests pass, web + runner + schemas typecheck clean, and the runner round-trips the new fields end-to-end (verified with a `POST /endpoints` smoke test against the live runner on `:8788`).

Here is what went wrong and how to run it.

### Why your command failed
- The workspace package is **`@leash/docs`** (with an **`s`**), not `@leash/doc`, so the filter matched nothing.
- That package defines **`dev`**, not **`start`** — it runs **`mintlify dev`**.

### Command to use
From the repo root:

```bash
pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev
```

Or from `apps/docs`:

```bash
cd apps/docs && pnpm dev
```

(`predev` runs schema doc generation, then Mintlify starts — usually **http://localhost:3000** for Mintlify; if that clashes with the Next app, Mintlify will pick another port and print it in the terminal.)

### MDX fixes (so Mintlify can parse the site)
Bare autolinks like `<http://localhost:3000>` break MDX (the parser treats `<` as JSX). Those were updated in **`apps/docs/quickstart.mdx`** and **`apps/docs/standards/x402-on-solana.mdx`** to normal markdown links so `mintlify dev` should get past the previous parse errors.

Run `pnpm --filter @leash/docs dev` again locally; you should see Mintlify’s “ready” URL in the log without the earlier parsing errors.

Three real questions in there. Let me answer the architecture one first, then fix the two SDK bugs that produced the misleading receipt.

## Who actually pays?

For your receipt above:

| Role | Address | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| Buyer agent (identity) | `33QvAYjE…JpeQrR` | The Metaplex Core **mint** of the buyer agent. Identity only. **Holds nothing, signs nothing.** |
| Buyer signer (Executive) | Your Privy embedded wallet | This is what actually **signs** the SPL transfer and **pays the USDC**. It’s acting as the agent’s Executive per the Metaplex “Run an Agent” model you chose. |
| Seller agent treasury | `CTd5VBFY…XNyox` | The PDA derived from the **seller** agent’s mint. This is the `payTo` — the receiver of the USDC. |
| SOL fee payer | `FYB56sVB…aLPZD` | The facilitator’s sponsor wallet. The buyer never pays SOL gas. |

So in the playground today: **your Privy wallet pays the USDC**. The buyer-agent Core asset is just the cryptographic identity that gets recorded on the receipt and that the policy gate (`evaluate`) is bound to. The Asset Signer PDA isn’t the fund source in this design — that was your explicit choice for the playground (“continue using Privy embedded wallet of user logged in for more security”).

If you ever want the agent’s treasury PDA itself to fund payments, that’s a different model (CPI through `Core Execute`), and we’d need to wire `@x402/svm`’s signer to a PDA-based signer that the executive co-signs for. We can do that later — for now the playground is correct as designed.

## The SDK bugs you spotted

1. **Receipt `price.amount = "1000"` is wrong.** The seller demanded `5000000` (5 USDC). The `1000` is `LIMITLESS_RULES.budget.perCall`, used as a fallback when there's no `PAYMENT-RESPONSE` header. That's a real bug — it lies on-record.
2. **No `reason` on the receipt** — the seller’s `payment-required` header carries an `error` field (`"insufficient_funds"`, `"facilitator_unreachable"`, etc.) and we’re ignoring it.
